proposals.title: Decoding Functional Units: A Systematic Search for Non-Genic, Lineage-Specific Functional Elements in Microbial Genomes
proposals.motivation: The central dogma of molecular biology posits genes as the primary functional units. However, pre-2026 genomic analyses reveal vast non-genic regions, and tools like Rfam show that structured RNAs can be functional without encoding proteins. The functional significance of lineage-specific, non-genic elements, especially in compact microbial genomes, remains systematically underexplored, limiting our understanding of genomic organization and evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant class of lineage-specific functional elements exists outside of annotated protein-coding genes, and that these elements can be systematically identified by searching for conserved, structured, and potentially translated sequences that defy standard gene models.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that integrates and extends Eddy lab methods. First, we will use Codetta to scan for non-canonical translation (e.g., alternative genetic codes, novel start sites) in intergenic regions. Second, we will apply ORFeus-like logic to detect other non-canonical translation events (e.g., ribosomal frameshifts, stop-codon readthroughs) in these regions. Third, we will use covariance models (as in Rfam) to search for conserved RNA secondary structures. Finally, we will apply independent set algorithms (as in benchmark construction papers) to create a non-redundant, high-confidence set of candidate functional elements from the union of these searches.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel catalog of candidate non-genic functional elements across diverse microbial lineages, providing testable hypotheses for experimental validation. It will contribute a new computational framework that moves beyond homology-based searches to *de novo* discovery of functional sequence, challenging the gene-centric view of genome function.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated application of tools designed for gene analysis (Codetta, ORFeus) to the non-genic space, combined with structural RNA detection, to systematically search for functional units that are invisible to standard gene finders and BLAST searches.

proposals.title: Somatic Sequence Evolution: A Computational Framework for Detecting and Characterizing Non-Germline Genomic Variation
proposals.motivation: The central dogma treats the genome as a static blueprint, yet somatic mutations accumulate in individual cells over a lifetime, contributing to aging, cancer, and potentially other diseases. While large-scale cancer genomics projects have catalogued somatic variants in tumors, there is no systematic, sensitive computational method to detect and analyze the full spectrum of somatic sequence changes—including small indels, structural variants, and non-canonical modifications—across diverse healthy and diseased tissues from the same individual. Pre-2026 single-cell sequencing technologies provide the raw data, but the analytical challenge of distinguishing true somatic changes from technical artifacts remains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying rigorous, phylogeny-aware comparative sequence analysis methods to multi-tissue, single-cell sequencing data from an individual, we can construct a high-confidence map of somatic genomic evolution, revealing lineage-specific mutations and patterns of sequence change that are invisible to germline-centric analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline building on Eddy lab expertise in sensitive sequence alignment (Infernal, HMMER) and error-aware analysis (Codetta, ORFeus). The core method will involve pairwise alignment of assembled contigs or read piles from single cells against the individual's diploid germline reference, using probabilistic models to distinguish sequencing errors from true low-frequency variants. We will employ phylogenetic independent set algorithms (as used for benchmark construction) to select maximally informative, high-quality cells for constructing somatic lineage trees. The search will be extended to detect non-canonical somatic changes, such as localized hypermutation or programmed rearrangements, by adapting models from Rfam and ribosomal frameshift detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source software toolkit for somatic variant discovery that sets a new standard for specificity and sensitivity beyond current ad-hoc methods. It will generate foundational insights into the rates and spectra of somatic mutation across human tissues, providing a baseline for studies of aging and pre-cancerous states. The framework will also enable the discovery of new classes of programmed or recurrent somatic sequence alterations.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing somatic variant callers focused on cancer or high-allele-frequency events, this approach leverages phylogenetic principles and the lab's deep expertise in detecting subtle, evolutionarily conserved signals to find rare, lineage-specific mutations across a whole organism. It applies the conceptual rigor of molecular evolution and comparative genomics, typically used for cross-species analysis, to the problem of variation within a single individual over time.

proposals.title: Decoding Non-Canonical Translation Variants: A Systematic Search for Programmed Ribosomal Frameshift-Associated Phenotypes in Human GWAS
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified thousands of genetic variants linked to human traits and diseases, but the functional interpretation of non-coding and synonymous variants remains a major challenge. A significant fraction of human genes contain sequences capable of inducing programmed ribosomal frameshifting (PRF) and other non-canonical translation events, which can alter protein function but are largely unexplored in population genetics. Linking specific genetic variation that modulates these events to phenotypic outcomes would open a new layer of functional genomics for disease mechanism discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic variants within putative programmed ribosomal frameshift (PRF) signals and other non-canonical translation elements are a functionally consequential but overlooked class of polymorphisms, and that systematic identification of such variants in GWAS loci will reveal novel mechanisms for human traits and diseases.
proposals.method: We will first compile a comprehensive set of human PRF and recoding signals using the ORFeus algorithm, extending its models with lineage-specific conservation patterns informed by Eddy lab expertise in homology and alignment (e.g., Rfam, Infernal). We will then intersect these functional elements with variant databases (e.g., gnomAD) and GWAS summary statistics. A key innovation will be the development of a new search method, inspired by Codetta's approach to genetic code prediction, to statistically associate specific variant types (e.g., those predicted to strengthen, weaken, or create de novo frameshift signals) with disease associations, moving beyond simple variant location.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated catalog of human genetic variation in non-canonical translation signals and its association with complex traits. It will deliver new, testable mechanistic hypotheses for GWAS loci currently lacking clear functional interpretation. The developed computational pipeline will establish a new best practice for integrating recoding-aware sequence analysis into population genomics.
proposals.novelty: While GWAS interpretation typically focuses on coding changes or regulatory motifs, this proposal uniquely focuses on variants affecting translational recoding, a specific mechanistic class largely invisible to standard annotation pipelines. The method's novelty lies in adapting sequence-analysis tools built for *detecting* recoding (ORFeus) into a framework for *associating* recoding-modulating variants with phenotypes.

proposals.title: Reference-aware compression of eukaryotic genomes using probabilistic models of lineage-specific variation
proposals.motivation: The exponential growth of genomic sequencing data has created a massive storage and transmission bottleneck. Current general-purpose compression tools (e.g., gzip) are inefficient for genomes, and even specialized genomic compressors often treat sequences as generic strings, failing to leverage the rich biological structure of homology, conserved gene families, and lineage-specific variation that is central to comparative genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a compression algorithm incorporating explicit probabilistic models of sequence evolution and homology, informed by a high-quality reference genome and multiple sequence alignments of conserved gene families, will achieve significantly higher compression ratios for eukaryotic genomes than existing methods, while preserving biologically meaningful features for downstream analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a compressor that first identifies and aligns conserved gene families (e.g., using HMMER/Rfam-like profile HMMs) from the target genome to a reference. For these regions, we will encode differences using a context-dependent model inspired by Codetta's codon-aware approach, but generalized to evolutionary substitution rates estimated from the alignment. For non-conserved, lineage-specific sequences, we will apply specialized models (e.g., PPM or LZMA) tuned for local nucleotide statistics. The core innovation is an integrated architecture where the compression model's parameters are themselves optimized by the algorithm based on the detected genomic features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source compression tool that reduces the practical costs of archiving and sharing genomic data. More importantly, it will demonstrate that compression efficiency is a powerful metric for validating biological models of sequence evolution and structure, creating a direct link between information theory and comparative genomics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing reference-based compressors which perform simple string differencing, our method uses explicit probabilistic models of biological sequence change (substitution, conservation) derived from homology searches, making the compression scheme inherently aware of the functional and evolutionary constraints shaping the genome.

proposals.title: Defining the Minimal Gene Set: A Computational and Phylogenetic Framework for Synthetic Genome Design
proposals.motivation: The quest for a minimal synthetic genome is a fundamental challenge in synthetic biology, aiming to define the core genetic requirements for life and enabling the construction of simplified, engineerable cells. Pre-2026 efforts, like the Mycoplasma mycoides JCVI-syn3.0, have produced reduced genomes but rely heavily on experimental trial-and-error, lacking a principled, predictive computational framework. A systematic, sequence-based method to define a minimal, functional genome from first principles is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal, viable genome can be computationally defined by integrating phylogenetic lineage analysis to identify universally conserved, essential gene families with specific, non-redundant functions, and by modeling non-canonical translation mechanisms to maximize coding density.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that first uses sensitive homology search (HMMER) against curated protein family databases (e.g., Pfam) to catalog gene families across a phylogenetically broad set of minimal bacterial lineages (e.g., Mycoplasma, Buchnera). An independent set algorithm will select the smallest set of families covering all essential cellular functions, avoiding functional redundancy. This core set will be analyzed with Codetta to verify a consistent genetic code and with ORFeus to identify potential overlapping genes and programmed frameshifts, allowing us to propose a maximally compressed nucleotide sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a rigorously defined, computationally predicted sequence for a minimal synthetic genome, providing a testable blueprint for experimental construction. It will advance the field by moving genome minimization from an empirical to a predictive discipline and will deliver new software tools for essential gene identification and genome compression analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of phylogenetic lineage analysis for essentiality with algorithms for detecting non-canonical translation, creating a principled, sequence-first design strategy rather than an iterative, experimental knockout approach.

proposals.title: FUN-SCAN: A Systematic, Homology-Based Pipeline for De Novo Identification of Functional Sequence Elements in Eukaryotic Genomes
proposals.motivation: The vast majority of eukaryotic genomes is non-coding, and current annotation pipelines are heavily biased towards protein-coding genes, often missing conserved non-coding RNAs, cis-regulatory modules, and other functional elements. A systematic, unbiased method to catalog all functional regions is needed to move beyond gene-centric models and understand genome biology. This is particularly urgent as large-scale sequencing projects generate genomes for diverse eukaryotic lineages with limited prior annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a multi-tiered, sensitive homology search strategy to whole genomes, we can systematically identify a more complete set of functional elements, including those missed by standard gene finders, by detecting evolutionarily conserved sequence and secondary structure signatures.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first uses Infernal and profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) from Rfam and Pfam to scan for known RNA families and protein domains. Second, we will employ sensitive, iterative homology search tools (e.g., HMMER3, jackhmmer) to detect divergent or lineage-specific homologs of known functional elements. Third, for regions with conservation but no homology hit, we will apply Codetta-like principles—using codon substitution patterns and sequence composition—to flag potential novel coding or non-canonical translation events (e.g., frameshifts) for experimental validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable, automated software pipeline for comprehensive functional element annotation, generating specific, testable catalogs for any eukaryotic genome. It will provide a richer, more accurate view of genomic functional content than standard gene annotation, directly testing the 'functional element' versus 'whole genome' analysis paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While tools exist for specific element types, a unified, homology-first pipeline that integrates RNA, protein, and non-canonical coding detection into a single systematic scan is novel. It repurposes and integrates Eddy lab methods (Infernal, HMMER, Codetta) for a new, overarching goal of de novo functional cartography.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Profile HMMs for Resolving Transposable Element Subfamily Diversity and Lineage-Specific Variation
proposals.motivation: Transposable elements (TEs) are major drivers of genome evolution and diversity, but their accurate annotation remains challenging. Current methods like RepeatMasker rely on consensus sequences or single profile HMMs per family, which often fail to capture the significant sequence divergence and structural variation among subfamilies and lineage-specific variants. This leads to incomplete or inaccurate TE annotation, hindering studies of their evolutionary dynamics and functional impact.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical HMM architecture, where a root HMM captures the conserved core of a TE family and specialized child HMMs model distinct subfamilies or structural variants, will significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity of TE detection and classification across diverse genomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that builds upon the HMMER3 framework. For a given TE family (e.g., from Dfam/Rfam), we will first construct a root profile HMM from a curated seed alignment. Using an iterative, tree-based clustering algorithm (e.g., based on sequence similarity and structural features), we will partition the family into distinct subfamilies. For each cluster, we will build a specialized child HMM. The search strategy will involve a two-pass scan: first with the root HMM for broad sensitivity, followed by the appropriate child HMM for precise subfamily classification, guided by a decision rule based on per-sequence scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more nuanced model for TE annotation that explicitly represents subfamily structure, leading to more accurate genome annotations. The resulting hierarchical HMM library will be a public resource, integrated into tools like Infernal, enabling researchers to trace TE lineage evolution and detect species-specific expansions with greater precision.
proposals.novelty: While profile HMMs are standard for gene families, their application to TEs has largely been 'one family, one HMM.' This proposal innovates by formally structuring the inherent diversity within a TE family into a hierarchical probabilistic model, moving beyond a single consensus to explicitly model the evolutionary lineages within the family.

proposals.title: De novo discovery and modeling of unannotated transposable element families using iterative HMM search and clustering
proposals.motivation: Repetitive elements (TEs) are major drivers of genome evolution and function, but comprehensive annotation remains incomplete. The widely used Repbase reference library is manually curated and known to be taxonomically biased, missing lineage-specific and low-copy-number families. This gap limits our understanding of genome architecture, regulatory innovation, and evolutionary history across diverse clades.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic, sensitive homology searches using profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) against whole-genome assemblies, followed by rigorous clustering, can identify and model novel, lineage-specific TE families absent from existing reference databases like Repbase.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the Dfam/Infernal methodology. Starting from a set of genomes of interest, we will perform an all-vs-all nhmmer search to identify repetitive regions. These hits will be clustered using a graph-based independent set algorithm (as used for benchmark construction) to define candidate families. For each cluster, we will build a consensus sequence, create a multiple alignment, and construct a covariance model (CM) or profile HMM. These new models will be used in an iterative search-refinement cycle to capture full family diversity. Novelty will be assessed by comparison to Repbase and Dfam 3.0 models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available collection of high-quality models for previously unannotated TE families, enriching the genomic 'parts list' for key lineages. It will provide a validated, automated pipeline for de novo TE discovery that complements manual curation, enabling more complete repeat annotation in new genome projects and comparative studies.
proposals.novelty: While HMMs are standard for searching known families, their systematic application for de novo discovery of TEs from raw genomes, using iterative model building and graph clustering to define robust families, represents a novel synthesis of Eddy lab tools (nhmmer, Infernal, clustering algorithms) for a specific, unsolved annotation problem.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Networks Reveal the Complex Lineage History of Transposable Element Families
proposals.motivation: Transposable element (TE) families evolve not as simple linear lineages but through a complex history of horizontal transfer, recombination, and differential selection. Standard phylogenetic tree models, which assume a single bifurcating history, are inadequate for capturing these reticulate evolutionary processes. A method to infer these complex histories from TE alignments is needed to accurately reconstruct their spread and impact on genome evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying phylogenetic network and coalescent models to multiple sequence alignments of TE families will reveal significant reticulate evolutionary signals, such as recombination and horizontal transfer events, that are obscured by traditional tree-based analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that takes curated TE alignments, such as those from Dfam or Rfam, as input. The core method will apply state-of-the-art phylogenetic network inference algorithms (e.g., using PhyloNet or SplitsTree) to model non-tree-like evolution. We will complement this with coalescent-based approaches (potentially using BEAST2) to estimate population genetic parameters like effective population size and recombination rates within TE families, validating signals against known biological mechanisms like horizontal transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for applying explicit phylogenetic network models to TE evolution, moving beyond descriptive similarity analyses. It will deliver new software tools and a revised understanding of the evolutionary dynamics within specific TE families, directly impacting studies of genome plasticity and mobile genetic element ecology.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic networks are used in species evolution and virology, their application to the deep, complex histories of TE families is novel. This approach moves beyond identifying TE families via homology search to explicitly modeling their genealogical incongruence and reticulate origins.

proposals.title: Dual: A computational model for predicting host silencing of transposable elements from sequence and epigenetic context
proposals.motivation: Transposable elements (TEs) are genomic parasites that must be epigenetically silenced by host genomes to maintain stability. While TE annotation tools like RepeatMasker identify TE-derived sequences, they do not predict the specific host repression mechanisms—such as DNA methylation, histone modifications, or small RNA targeting—that act upon them. Understanding the sequence and epigenetic signatures that determine these silencing fates is crucial for deciphering genome regulation and evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific combination of TE family, its sequence features (e.g., k-mer composition, target site duplications), and its genomic context (e.g., proximity to genes, CpG islands) can be used to predict the primary host silencing mechanism employed at each locus.
proposals.method: We will develop Dual, a computational model that integrates TE annotations with pre-2026 epigenetic data (e.g., ENCODE/Roadmap Epigenomics ChIP-seq, bisulfite-seq, and small RNA-seq). For each TE instance, we will extract a feature vector including family identity (from Rfam/Repbase homology), local sequence motifs, and chromatin state segmentations. A supervised machine learning classifier (e.g., a random forest or gradient boosting model) will be trained to predict the dominant silencing signal from the feature vector, building on the group's expertise in sequence analysis and model training as seen in Codetta and ORFeus.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first genome-wide predictive map linking TE instances to their likely repression mechanisms. It will provide a new tool for generating testable hypotheses about TE regulation and offer insights into how silencing pathways evolve in response to specific TE lineages. The model and its feature importance analysis will reveal sequence determinants of epigenetic fate.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools annotate TE presence, Dual uniquely predicts the *host's regulatory response* to that presence. It moves beyond cataloging TE-derived sequence to modeling the host-TE interaction as a dual system defined by specific repression mechanisms.

proposals.title: Infernal2: De Novo Construction of Covariance Models from Sequence Alignments Without Structural Annotation
proposals.motivation: Covariance Models (CMs) are powerful for RNA homology search, but their construction in Infernal relies on input alignments annotated with consensus secondary structure. This dependency limits their application to the vast majority of sequence families without known or confidently predicted structures. A method to build accurate CMs from sequence alignments alone would dramatically expand the scope of non-coding RNA discovery and analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That a probabilistic model integrating co-evolutionary signal and thermodynamic plausibility can infer the consensus secondary structure directly from a sequence alignment, enabling the construction of CMs with accuracy comparable to those built from structurally annotated seeds.
proposals.method: We will develop a new algorithm that extends the core CM architecture. It will treat the consensus secondary structure as a hidden variable, iteratively optimizing it alongside CM emission and transition parameters using a maximum likelihood framework. The optimization will be constrained by a prior derived from thermodynamic folding stability (e.g., via a ViennaRNA-like model) and informed by mutual information scores from the alignment. The search will employ a stochastic, grammar-aware algorithm (e.g., a specialized Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampler) to navigate the space of possible consensus structures.
proposals.contribution: This will create a fully automated pipeline for building CMs from any multiple sequence alignment, removing a major bottleneck in the Rfam pipeline. It will enable the systematic modeling of putative RNA families from metagenomic and genomic surveys where structural data is absent, significantly expanding the utility of CMs for de novo non-coding RNA gene discovery.
proposals.novelty: While tools like R-scape assess significance of covariation in given structures, and RNAalifold predicts consensus structures, no method integrates a full CM parameter estimation with simultaneous, probabilistically rigorous inference of the consensus RNA secondary structure directly from the alignment.

proposals.title: Direct Covariance Model Search on Raw Sequencing Reads for High-Sensitivity Non-Coding RNA Discovery
proposals.motivation: Metagenomic and transcriptomic sequencing projects generate vast amounts of raw read and contig data, but current analysis pipelines rely on assembly or alignment to reference genomes, which discards non-aligning or low-abundance signals. Covariance Models (CMs) are powerful probabilistic models for structured non-coding RNA (ncRNA) families, but their application is bottlenecked by the requirement for full-length, assembled sequences, limiting their utility for discovering novel ncRNAs directly from complex, fragmented sequencing data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the CM search algorithm to operate efficiently on short, error-prone sequencing reads and unassembled contigs, we can achieve a significant increase in sensitivity for detecting remote homologs and novel members of structured RNA families in metagenomic and transcriptomic datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a new tool, 'ReadCM', that modifies the CM dynamic programming algorithms (e.g., the HMMER3-like accelerated profile HMM/CM pipeline) to handle gapped, partial alignments typical of short reads. The core innovation is a banded DP strategy that constrains the search space for reads while preserving the CM's sensitivity to secondary structure. We will integrate quality score information for error-tolerant matching and implement a fragment-based scoring system to aggregate evidence across multiple reads mapping to the same genomic locus, analogous to methods used in ORFeus for frameshift detection. The search will be optimized using the Infernal toolkit's existing infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first efficient method for applying rigorous RNA family models directly to raw sequencing data, bypassing assembly biases. It will enable the discovery of novel structured RNAs from fragmented data, such as viral metagenomes or degraded samples, and improve the annotation of RNA genes in incomplete draft genomes. The tool will extend the Rfam-centric analysis paradigm to the raw data stage.
proposals.novelty: While CMs are established for searching genomes and contigs, applying them directly to short reads is non-trivial due to computational complexity and the challenge of scoring partial structures. This approach is novel compared to existing read-classification tools (e.g., Kraken2) that use k-mers and lack sensitivity for remote homology and structural RNA features.

proposals.title: MegaHMMer: Scalable, HMM-based homology search for petabase-scale metagenomic assemblies
proposals.motivation: The scale of public metagenomic assembled genome (MAG) databases is entering the petabase era, driven by projects like the Earth BioGenome Project and large-scale microbiome studies. Existing homology search tools, including HMMER3, are not optimized for querying this volume of fragmented, diverse, and often incomplete genomic data, creating a bottleneck for discovering novel gene families and tracing their evolutionary lineage across the microbial tree of life.
proposals.hypothesis: By implementing a novel, disk-based, partitioned search architecture and integrating pre-filtering techniques from protein search (like MMseqs2's k-mer scoring), we can enable sensitive profile hidden Markov model searches against petabase-scale MAG databases with practical computational resources.
proposals.method: We will develop MegaHMMer, extending the core algorithms of HMMER. The system will partition massive target databases (e.g., from GenBank, IMG/M) into shards based on sequence composition or taxonomy, enabling distributed search. For each shard, we will implement a two-stage filter: a rapid k-mer pre-screening step to reject clear negatives, followed by the full HMMER3 acceleration pipeline (MSV, bias, Viterbi). A new disk-backed sequence fetch and alignment module will replace in-memory storage, allowing iterative processing of results from massive databases without loading them entirely into RAM.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the community with the first tool capable of running sensitive, HMM-based homology searches against entire petabase MAG repositories on a moderate compute cluster. It will enable systematic annotation of gene families across the planetary microbiome and facilitate the discovery of novel, lineage-specific genes in uncultivated organisms.
proposals.novelty: While scalable protein-protein search exists, no tool adapts the sensitive probabilistic framework of HMMER to petabase-scale *nucleotide* (assembled contig) databases. The novelty lies in the hybrid architecture combining HMMER's rigorous domain detection with scalable database partitioning and disk-based I/O management.

proposals.title: Automated, Data-Driven Curation of Gathering Thresholds for Homology Detection in Rfam and Pfam
proposals.motivation: Manually curated gathering thresholds (GA thresholds) are critical for controlling the sensitivity and specificity of homology searches in databases like Rfam and Pfam. The current process is labor-intensive, subjective, and difficult to scale, leading to inconsistencies and delayed updates as new sequence data emerges. Automating this curation would improve database quality, accelerate the annotation of new genomes, and free expert time for higher-level analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that family-specific GA thresholds can be accurately predicted using a model trained on existing expert-curated thresholds, sequence features, and search statistics, enabling a reproducible and scalable curation pipeline.
proposals.method: We will develop a supervised learning model using features derived from multiple sequence alignments (e.g., sequence diversity, information content, length), HMMER3 search statistics (e.g., score distributions), and existing curated GA thresholds. The model will be trained on the expertly curated thresholds in Rfam and Pfam. We will implement an automated pipeline that, for a new alignment, generates a proposed threshold and provides supporting diagnostics (e.g., score histograms, potential outliers) for expert review, integrating this into the existing Infernal/HMMER infrastructure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-source tool for semi-automated GA threshold curation, directly benefiting the Rfam and Pfam databases. It will establish a reproducible standard for threshold setting, reduce curator workload, and enable more frequent database updates to keep pace with genomic sequencing.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on *using* GA thresholds for search, this project directly addresses the *generation* of these thresholds, applying machine learning to formalize and automate expert curation practice. It leverages the deep, pre-existing curation within Rfam/Pfam as training data, a resource unique to this lab.

proposals.title: Structure-First Homology: Deriving Sequence Families from 3D Structural Clustering to Expand Functional Annotation
proposals.motivation: Traditional homology search, as implemented in tools like HMMER and databases like Pfam/Rfam, is sequence-first, limiting discovery to relatives within detectable sequence similarity. This misses deeply divergent homologs where sequence signal is eroded but 3D structure is conserved, a critical gap for understanding ancient protein and RNA lineages. The rapid growth of the Protein Data Bank (PDB) and RNA 3D structure archives now provides a sufficient foundation to invert this workflow.
proposals.hypothesis: Clustering macromolecular structures by 3D similarity, independent of sequence, will yield coherent functional families that encompass members with no statistically significant sequence similarity, thereby revealing more complete evolutionary lineages.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline, 'Stratigraph', that first clusters all solved protein and RNA structures in the PDB/NDB using efficient, alignment-free 3D similarity metrics (e.g., DaliLite, 3D-BLAST-SS). For each resulting structural cluster, we will extract the associated sequences and build a multiple sequence alignment, from which we will construct a probabilistic profile (HMM for proteins, CM for RNAs). These structure-derived models will be benchmarked against sequence-derived families (e.g., Pfam clans) and used to search sequence databases to recruit new members.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel resource of structure-defined homology families, systematically expanding the known membership of ancient protein folds and RNA motifs. It will provide a new class of sensitive search models for functional annotation that are blind to sequence bias, directly testing the limits of sequence-based homology.
proposals.novelty: While structural similarity is used to validate homology, it is not the primary, initiating criterion for building large-scale family databases. This inverts the canonical bioinformatics workflow, using structure as the independent variable to define homology, a conceptually different approach from post-hoc structural comparison of sequence families.

proposals.title: Functional Divergence in Sequence Families: A Systematic Census and Mechanistic Analysis
proposals.motivation: Standard homology-based annotation assumes function is conserved within protein and RNA families, but this assumption is known to fail in specific cases, leading to widespread annotation errors. A systematic, large-scale identification of families where sequence similarity is decoupled from molecular function is lacking, limiting our understanding of evolutionary mechanisms and the reliability of genomic databases.
proposals.hypothesis: That a significant fraction of curated sequence families in databases like Pfam and Rfam contain subfamilies or individual members with divergent molecular functions, and that these cases can be identified through integrated analysis of sequence, structure, and genomic context signals.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline to analyze families from Pfam and Rfam. First, we will use sensitive profile HMMs (e.g., HMMER) to define families and subfamilies. For each, we will integrate orthogonal functional signals: predicted genetic codes (using Codetta), genomic neighborhood (operon/phylogenetic profiling), predicted non-canonical translation (using ORFeus), and inferred 3D structure (via AlphaFold2 and ESMFold). Statistical discordance between these signals and the dominant family annotation will flag candidates for functional divergence, which will be manually curated and experimentally validated in collaboration with wet-lab partners.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first curated database of 'functionally divergent' sequence families, providing a crucial resource for improving automated annotation. It will yield new biological insights into the evolutionary pathways that lead to functional innovation within conserved sequence scaffolds and establish best practices for detecting such cases.
proposals.novelty: While individual cases of functional divergence are known, a systematic census using an integrated, multi-signal approach—particularly incorporating state-of-the-art structural prediction and non-canonical translation detection—is novel. This moves beyond simple sequence similarity thresholds to a mechanistic model of function prediction.

proposals.title: Detecting Phylogenetic Outliers in Metagenomic Protein Space: A Transfer Anomaly Approach to Novel Domain Discovery
proposals.motivation: Metagenomic sequencing reveals vast, unexplored sequence space, but standard homology search tools like HMMER are biased towards known families in databases like Pfam, potentially missing deeply divergent or truly novel protein domains. This limits our understanding of the functional and evolutionary diversity of life on Earth. Identifying sequences that are genuine outliers, not just remote homologs, is a critical unsolved problem in computational genomics.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying statistical outlier detection methods to the embedding spaces generated by deep learning protein language models, we can distinguish sequences that are merely remote homologs of known families from those that represent architecturally novel protein domains with no detectable homology.
proposals.method: We will generate protein family multiple sequence alignments and profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) for known domains from Pfam. Using a pre-trained protein language model (e.g., ESM-1b or ProtTrans), we will embed both known family members and unannotated metagenomic sequences into a shared vector space. We will then apply transfer anomaly detection algorithms, such as Isolation Forest or One-Class SVM, trained on the embeddings of known families to score metagenomic sequences. High-confidence outliers will be clustered and analyzed for conserved secondary structure and residue patterns to define candidate novel families.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a computational pipeline for the systematic discovery of novel protein domains that evade current homology-based methods. It will contribute a benchmark dataset of high-confidence outlier sequences and candidate novel families from large-scale metagenomic surveys like Tara Oceans and the Earth Microbiome Project, expanding the map of protein sequence space.
proposals.novelty: While anomaly detection is used in other fields, its application to find novel protein domains by leveraging the semantic embeddings from protein language models, rather than raw sequence similarity, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond the paradigm of homology search to a model-based definition of sequence 'normality' for outlier detection.

proposals.title: Biological Context Search (BioConSearch): A Query-Aware, Multi-Relational Network Model for Ranking Homology Search Results
proposals.motivation: Current sequence database search tools like BLAST and HMMER rank hits primarily by alignment scores and E-values, treating each hit independently. This fails to capture the rich biological context embedded in the ensemble of results, such as whether a hit represents a conserved ortholog, a recent paralog, or a domain-specific family member. Consequently, users must manually sift through long lists to find the biologically most relevant matches, a major bottleneck in genomic analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling distinct biological relationships (e.g., orthology, co-domain membership, structural similarity) as separate edge types in a multi-relational network of search hits, and by using a query-conditioned ranking model, we can significantly improve the ranking of biologically relevant sequences over methods based solely on pairwise similarity scores.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-relational network from the top-N hits of a sensitive HMMER or DIAMOND search. Edge types will be derived from pre-2026 resources: orthology (eggNOG, OrthoDB), domain architecture (Pfam, InterPro), and structural similarity (fold-level predictions from DeepFRI). A neural ranking model, such as a Graph Attention Network (GAT) with relational edges, will be trained on curated benchmarks from the CAFA and Critical Assessment of Genome Annotation (CAGI) challenges. The model will be conditioned on features of the query sequence (e.g., domain content, taxonomic lineage) to dynamically weight evidence from different network layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, open-source search result ranking system that integrates diverse biological databases into a unified relevance model. It will provide a significant practical advance for genome annotation and comparative genomics by automating the identification of functionally informative homologs. The resulting benchmarks and models will also serve as a framework for evaluating biological context in sequence analysis.
proposals.novelty: While network propagation methods like NetGO exist, they apply a static, global model. Our approach is novel in its explicit modeling of multiple, semantically distinct biological relationships and its use of a query-aware machine learning model to perform dynamic, context-sensitive ranking tailored to the specific search instance.

proposals.title: Co-similarity network ranking: contextual prioritization of sequence homology search hits
proposals.motivation: Current sequence database search tools like BLAST rank hits primarily by pairwise alignment scores (E-value, bit-score), which can obscure the biological context. A high-scoring hit may be an isolated outlier or a fragment, while a moderately scoring hit may be a central, conserved member of a large protein family. A ranking method that considers the global structure of similarity among all results could better identify functionally important homologs, directly addressing challenges in gene annotation and protein family characterization prevalent in Eddy's recent work on genetic codes and frameshifts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ranking database hits by their centrality in a sequence similarity network, analogous to ranking papers by co-citation in bibliometrics, will prioritize biologically core members of protein families more effectively than ranking by pairwise alignment scores alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that takes a BLAST or HMMER results table and constructs a network where nodes are database sequences. Edges will be drawn if the pairwise alignment E-value between sequences passes a significance threshold. We will then adapt bibliographic coupling and co-citation algorithms—specifically, we will compute a 'co-similarity' score for each node based on the connectivity patterns of its neighbors (e.g., using a weighted PageRank or identifying clusters with community detection like the Louvain method). The final rank will integrate this network centrality measure with the original alignment score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, context-aware ranking algorithm for sequence homology searches, implemented as an open-source tool. It will contribute a new perspective on interpreting search results, potentially improving the accuracy of functional inference and the identification of key representatives for building alignment profiles, directly benefiting efforts like Rfam and Codetta.
proposals.novelty: While network methods exist for protein-protein interaction data, applying bibliometric-inspired co-citation analysis directly to the graph of sequence similarities among database hits is a novel conceptual analogy. It moves beyond the standard model of independent, pairwise evaluation of hits.

proposals.title: StreamRank: Real-Time Contextual Ranking of Homology Matches in Streaming Sequence Data
proposals.motivation: High-throughput sequencing technologies generate continuous streams of data, such as from environmental sensors or clinical monitors. Current search tools (e.g., BLAST, HMMER) treat matches in isolation, lacking the ability to prioritize results based on their collective, evolving biological context in real time. This creates a bottleneck for applications like pathogen surveillance, where the significance of a viral sequence depends on its prevalence and relatedness to other sequences in the current sample stream.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying incremental, network-based ranking algorithms—inspired by PageRank—to a dynamically constructed sequence similarity graph will enable the real-time identification of the most contextually significant matches in a data stream, improving the sensitivity and prioritization for downstream analysis.
proposals.method: We will build a system that, for each new sequence in a stream, performs a fast homology search (using profile HMMs from Rfam/Pfam or k-mer sketches) to find matches against a reference database. Each significant match will become a node in a rolling graph, with edges weighted by alignment scores. We will implement an online version of a random-walk with restart algorithm to update importance scores incrementally as the graph changes. The system will output a continuously ranked list of sequence matches, prioritizing those with high network centrality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for context-aware, online ranking of biological sequence search results. It will yield a scalable, open-source pipeline for intelligent filtering of streaming genomic data, directly applicable to real-time metagenomic classification and outbreak detection. The method will advance beyond static batch analysis to dynamic, data-driven prioritization.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of streaming graph algorithms from web search with biological sequence analysis is novel. While PageRank has been used for static biological networks, applying its incremental variants to a real-time, evolving sequence similarity graph for hit prioritization is an unexplored approach.

proposals.title: Adversarial Benchmarking of Profile HMMs via Federated Fitness Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) are foundational for sensitive homology detection, yet systematic evaluation of their specificity remains challenging. Current benchmarks, like those constructed from independent sets, test sensitivity but often lack the adversarial sequences needed to probe subtle biases where models may incorrectly assign high scores to unrelated families. A rigorous test for selectivity is crucial for improving methods like HMMER and Pfam/Rfam.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial sequence generator, trained against a federated network of diverse HMM databases, can produce synthetic benchmark sequences that uniquely stress-test the specificity of a target profile HMM, revealing systematic failure modes not captured by natural sequence benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a central generator using a deep reinforcement learning or evolutionary algorithm, where the reward function is the differential score between the target HMM and a panel of background HMMs. The background panel will be constructed via federated queries: candidate sequences are broadcast to participating servers hosting distinct HMM databases (e.g., institutional Pfam mirrors, custom family sets), returning aggregated E-values. This federated fitness landscape will guide the generator to refine sequences towards high target specificity or, conversely, to expose promiscuous high-scoring artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, high-quality benchmark set for HMM selectivity, complementing existing sensitivity benchmarks. It will yield a public software platform for federated adversarial benchmarking and may identify systematic biases in widely used profile models, informing future improvements to HMM construction and scoring.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its adversarial, generative objective and its federated architecture, which leverages distributed, potentially private HMM databases to define specificity—a task previously limited by the scope of any single public database.

proposals.title: Adversarial Optimization of Profile HMMs for Negative Design in Sequence Analysis
proposals.motivation: Profile HMMs are powerful tools for modeling sequence families and identifying homologs, but standard training maximizes sensitivity to a positive set, often leading to unwanted matches to evolutionarily related or structurally similar off-target sequences. This is a critical problem in applications like designing RNA aptamers to avoid genomic off-targets or engineering protein domains to exclude specific interaction motifs, where specificity must be actively engineered, not just observed. Current methods lack a principled framework to explicitly optimize an HMM against a defined negative sequence set while preserving its core modeling function.
proposals.hypothesis: A profile HMM's parameters can be directly optimized via a constrained adversarial loss function to simultaneously achieve high likelihood for positive training sequences and low probability for designated negative sequences, thereby creating a model with engineered specificity.
proposals.method: We will develop an optimization procedure using gradient-based methods on the HMM parameters (match state emissions, transitions). The core loss function will combine: (1) the negative log-likelihood of positive sequences, (2) a penalty term for high log-likelihood of negative sequences, and (3) a Kullback-Leibler divergence regularizer to keep the model close to a sensible initial parameterization (e.g., from HMMER's hmmbuild). We will implement this using automatic differentiation (e.g., in PyTorch) to compute gradients through the Forward algorithm, enabling efficient search within a constrained, plausible parameter space.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool for the 'negative design' of sequence models, allowing researchers to engineer HMMs for tasks requiring explicit avoidance of specific sequences. It will extend the utility of profile HMMs from descriptive family models to prescriptive design tools for synthetic biology and precise molecular diagnostics. The software will integrate with the HMMER suite, making it accessible for practical use.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is known in machine learning, its application to profile HMMs for explicit negative sequence avoidance is novel. This moves beyond standard discriminative training or filtering, directly embedding avoidance constraints into the generative model's parameters through a constrained optimization framework.

proposals.title: ClanFold: Defining Protein Evolutionary Clans from Tertiary Structure Using Graph Clustering
proposals.motivation: Protein families are traditionally defined by sequence homology, which fails to detect deep evolutionary relationships where sequence similarity is erased. While structural similarity often reveals these distant homologies, current structural classification databases (e.g., SCOP, CATH) are manually curated and do not provide a systematic, automated method for grouping all known structures into exhaustive, hierarchical 'clans' based purely on 3D shape. An automated, scalable method is needed to map the full landscape of structural relationships, independent of sequence.
proposals.hypothesis: That an all-vs-all structural similarity network of known protein domains, constructed using fast, alignment-free structural comparison methods, can be partitioned into discrete, hierarchically nested clusters (clans) that reveal evolutionary relationships invisible to sequence analysis.
proposals.method: We will build a graph from all protein domains in the PDB and AlphaFold DB (pre-2026 releases), using the TM-score from the US-align algorithm for rapid, accurate structural alignment as the primary edge weight. We will apply the Markov Cluster Algorithm (MCL) to partition this graph into initial, non-overlapping clans based on structural similarity density. Subsequently, we will use hierarchical clustering on the same graph to define nested sub-clan relationships. The pipeline will be validated against manually curated superfamilies in SCOP and tested for its ability to group known divergent families (e.g., different Rossmann fold lineages) and putative new relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, automated, and publicly accessible classification of protein structures into evolutionary clans defined solely by 3D similarity. It will provide a new resource for protein function prediction, fold evolution studies, and the identification of potential remote homology targets for experimental characterization. The method will complement sequence-based clan databases like Pfam.
proposals.novelty: While structural comparison tools exist, their systematic application to build a complete, hierarchical clan taxonomy automatically is novel. This approach explicitly bypasses sequence information, a departure from Eddy's prior work on sequence families (e.g., Rfam, Pfam), to discover relationships at the extreme limits of evolutionary divergence.

proposals.title: ClanFinder: Automated Discovery and Annotation of Non-Homologous RNA Families from Expanding Sequence Databases
proposals.motivation: The Rfam database catalogs RNA families via covariance models (CMs), grouping evolutionarily related but non-homologous families into 'clans' based on structural similarity. Current clan annotation is a manual, expert-driven process that cannot scale with the exponential growth of metagenomic sequence data. This bottleneck prevents the systematic organization of newly discovered RNA families into higher-order evolutionary and functional groups, limiting our understanding of deep RNA lineage relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an automated computational pipeline, leveraging sensitive all-vs-all structural comparisons and graph-based clustering, can accurately propose new RNA clans from novel families discovered in large-scale sequence searches, recapitulating expert annotations and revealing previously unrecognized deep evolutionary connections.
proposals.method: We will develop ClanFinder, a pipeline that first uses Infernal's cmscan to place sequences into known Rfam families. For unannotated sequences, we will build new CMs and perform all-vs-all comparisons using CMsearch. We will compute pairwise structural similarity scores between CMs, integrating metrics like SCOPE's structural alignment score and sequence overlap. These scores will define a weighted graph of families, which we will cluster using a community detection algorithm (e.g., the Leiden method) to propose clan memberships. The pipeline will output ranked clan proposals with supporting evidence for expert review.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully automated system for proposing RNA clan structures, dramatically accelerating the curation of Rfam. It will provide a scalable framework for organizing the deluge of novel RNA families from metagenomics into an evolutionary context. The method and resulting clan proposals will be a key resource for studying the deep evolutionary history and functional diversification of structured non-coding RNAs.
proposals.novelty: While tools exist for family discovery (Infernal) and pairwise structural comparison (SCOPE, R-scape), no pre-2026 method automates the higher-order task of clan inference by integrating these into a systematic, graph-based clustering framework designed for the specific biological problem of grouping non-homologous RNA families.

proposals.title: A Systematic Benchmark for Protein-Coding Gene Annotation Reveals a Core, Method-Independent Gene Set
proposals.motivation: Current estimates of protein-coding gene counts in model genomes vary widely due to differing annotation methods and criteria, creating ambiguity in comparative genomics and functional analysis. This fluidity undermines the concept of a definitive gene catalog, which is a fundamental assumption in evolutionary and systems biology. A rigorous, method-agnostic assessment is needed to distinguish core, reliably annotated genes from the fluid annotation 'fringe'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of protein-coding genes can be identified across diverse annotation methods, and that this core set is stable and definitive, while variation occurs primarily in a subset of genes with weak or ambiguous sequence signatures.
proposals.method: We will apply a suite of independent, state-of-the-art gene-finding methods—including homology-based tools like HMMER (used in Pfam/Rfam searches), ab initio predictors, and ribosome profiling-informed models—to a curated set of eukaryotic genomes. Following principles from benchmark construction work (e.g., independent set algorithms), we will treat each method's output as an independent annotation and identify the consensus core gene set. We will specifically analyze discrepancies using Codetta-like analyses for alternative genetic codes and ORFeus-like detection of non-canonical translation to see if they explain annotation differences.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available benchmark dataset of 'core' versus 'fluid' gene annotations for key genomes, providing a new standard for evaluating gene prediction tools. It will shift the discourse from debating a single 'true' gene count to understanding the biological and technical reasons for annotation variance, offering a more nuanced framework for genomic comparison.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks exist, none systematically decouple methodological artifact from biological reality to define a stable, method-independent gene core. The approach uniquely integrates diverse detection paradigms (homology, sequence signal, translation evidence) not as a consensus pipeline, but as independent estimators to triangulate a robust gene set.

proposals.title: Systematic functional annotation of mouse protein-coding and non-coding genes through integrative homology and motif analysis
proposals.motivation: The mouse genome is a premier model for mammalian biology, yet a significant fraction of its protein-coding genes and the vast majority of its non-coding RNA genes lack precise functional annotation. This gap limits the interpretability of genetic and genomic studies in this foundational organism. Current automated pipelines often rely on single lines of evidence, leading to incomplete or low-confidence annotations, particularly for genes with non-canonical features or those lacking clear orthologs in well-studied lineages.
proposals.hypothesis: An integrative computational pipeline, combining sensitive homology search, conserved domain detection, and non-canonical translation signal analysis, can assign high-confidence, specific molecular functions to a substantially larger fraction of mouse genes than existing methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline that first uses profile hidden Markov models from Pfam and Rfam for sensitive domain and RNA family detection. For protein-coding genes, we will integrate Codetta-like analysis of genetic code signatures and ORFeus-like scanning for programmed frameshifts to refine coding potential and identify unusual translation events. For genes lacking strong homology, we will employ covariance models and de novo motif discovery to infer functional RNA structures and regulatory elements. All evidence will be combined using a probabilistic scoring framework to generate confidence-ranked functional predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a comprehensive, publicly available functional annotation resource for the mouse genome, significantly improving the biological interpretability of mouse genetic data. It will also serve as a methodological blueprint for applying deep, integrative sequence analysis to other complex eukaryotic genomes.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely integrates Eddy lab methods for detecting non-canonical translation (ORFeus) and genetic code prediction (Codetta) with established homology search techniques to tackle both canonical and atypical genes. This moves beyond simple BLAST-based transfers to a multi-evidence, model-based annotation system specifically tuned for mammalian genomic complexity.

proposals.title: OrthoNet: A unified machine learning framework for orthology prediction integrating sequence, synteny, and expression data
proposals.motivation: Accurate ortholog identification is fundamental for comparative genomics and functional annotation, but current methods (e.g., reciprocal best BLAST hits, tree-based reconciliation) often rely on single data types and struggle with complex evolutionary scenarios like gene duplications and horizontal transfer. The integration of heterogeneous evidence—sequence homology, genomic context (synteny), and expression patterns—remains an ad-hoc, unsolved challenge, limiting the reliability of downstream analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a supervised machine learning classifier, trained on high-confidence benchmark sets, can effectively integrate sequence, synteny, and gene expression features to achieve higher accuracy and robustness in ortholog prediction than methods based on any single data type.
proposals.method: We will construct a gold-standard training set using independent set algorithms, as in Eddy's recent correction paper, to select non-redundant, high-confidence orthologs from existing databases. Features will include sequence similarity scores and alignment statistics (leveraging HMMER/Rfam-like profile searches), conserved synteny blocks quantified by genomic distance metrics, and correlation of tissue- or condition-specific expression profiles from public RNA-seq data. We will implement and compare several classifiers, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost) and a deep neural network, to integrate these features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, open-source orthology prediction tool that provides more reliable assignments, especially in paralog-rich gene families and deep evolutionary comparisons. It will also deliver a novel, multi-evidence benchmark dataset for the community, advancing the systematic evaluation of orthology methods.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools use synteny or expression as post-filters, OrthoNet will be a unified, trainable model that learns the relative weights of these evidence types directly from data, moving beyond heuristic combinations. This approach directly extends Eddy's expertise in sequence analysis and benchmark construction into integrative, machine learning-driven genomics.

proposals.title: Deconstructing Functional RNA Families: An Inverse Alignment Approach to Identify Minimal Essential Sequence
proposals.motivation: Current methods like Rfam identify conserved sequence families by aligning homologs, but the resulting consensus models often include regions of uncertain functional necessity. This complicates the design of functional assays and obscures the core sequence determinants of activity. A systematic, computational method to identify the minimal essential conserved sequence would clarify the genotype-to-phenotype map for non-coding RNAs and other functional elements.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given functionally defined RNA family, the minimal sequence required for that function can be identified by iteratively deleting non-conserved regions from a structural alignment and testing the functional viability of the resulting sequences in silico, guided by evolutionary conservation and structural constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm, 'Infernal-Reduce', that takes a high-quality Rfam covariance model (CM) as input. Using the alignment and its inferred consensus secondary structure, the method will systematically mask or delete columns with low conservation scores and high positional entropy. It will then use the Infernal framework's cmscore to evaluate whether the reduced model still significantly detects all true family members, simulating a functional knockout. The process will iterate, employing a heuristic search akin to Codetta's code-finding logic but applied to column deletion, to find the minimal column set retaining family-wide detection.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new computational tool for the deconstruction of RNA families, yielding 'minimal essential models' that highlight core functional nucleotides. It will provide specific, testable hypotheses for mutational studies and could refine the annotation of functional boundaries in genomes. The method will extend the Eddy lab's suite of tools for analyzing sequence-function relationships.
proposals.novelty: While alignment identifies what is conserved, this inverse approach actively determines what conservation is essential for family membership, a distinct and more aggressive form of model distillation. It applies an 'independent set' deletion logic, reminiscent of benchmark construction, to the columns of a probabilistic model rather than to sequences.

proposals.title: Modeling Long-Range Dependencies in RNA and Protein Families with Conditional Random Fields
proposals.motivation: Current profile models for sequence families (e.g., profile HMMs, covariance models) assume conditional independence between sequence positions given the hidden state path, limiting their ability to capture direct, long-range functional dependencies. These dependencies are crucial for modeling RNA tertiary interactions, allosteric couplings in proteins, and compensatory substitutions in conserved structural elements, but remain poorly integrated into standard homology search and alignment tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling pairwise dependencies between non-adjacent residues in a probabilistic graphical model framework will significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity of remote homology detection and the accuracy of multiple sequence alignments for families with strong long-range constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop new models based on Conditional Random Fields (CRFs), extending the architecture of profile HMMs and covariance models. The core innovation is the addition of a sparse set of edges between non-adjacent match states, with parameters learned via regularized maximum likelihood from trusted alignments. Inference will use loopy belief propagation, and search will employ a modified version of the HMMER/Infernal pipeline. We will benchmark against existing tools (HMMER, Infernal, HH-suite) using carefully constructed independent test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, practical software tool for sequence analysis that captures a broader spectrum of evolutionary and functional constraints. It will provide a more accurate statistical framework for annotating non-coding RNAs and protein domains where long-range interactions define the functional fold, directly impacting genome interpretation.
proposals.novelty: While pairwise CRFs have been explored in theory, a robust, scalable implementation for genome-scale homology search, integrated into the proven HMMER/Infernal paradigm, represents a significant and non-trivial advance over the independently-emitting models that dominate the field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Joint Bayesian Inference of Sequence Homology and Phylogenetic Topology with Probabilistic Graphical Models
proposals.motivation: Current homology search and alignment methods, like those in the Rfam pipeline, rely on a fixed, known phylogenetic tree to inform sequence models and break alignment ties. This is a major limitation when analyzing sequences from poorly characterized lineages or from metagenomic data, where the evolutionary relationships are uncertain or unknown. A method that co-infers homology and tree structure would be more robust and broadly applicable across the tree of life.
proposals.hypothesis: A probabilistic model that treats the phylogenetic tree topology as a latent variable to be inferred simultaneously with sequence alignment and family membership will yield more accurate homology assignments and alignments in cases of deep or uncertain evolutionary relationships.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian graphical model where observed sequences are leaves of an unknown tree. The model will integrate a tree prior (e.g., Kingman's coalescent), a substitution model along branches, and an insertion/deletion process. Inference will be performed using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), with proposals that alter the tree topology (e.g., subtree pruning and regrafting) alongside alignment paths. Computational efficiency will be addressed by adapting the banded dynamic programming techniques from tools like Infernal and HMMER to work within the MCMC framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, open-source software tool for probabilistic phylogenetic-aware homology search, removing the dependency on a pre-specified tree. It will provide a formal statistical framework for quantifying uncertainty in both tree topology and alignment, directly benefiting studies of horizontal gene transfer, orphan genes, and deep microbial phylogeny.
proposals.novelty: While joint alignment and tree inference exists in phylogenetics (e.g., BAli-Phy), it is not applied to the *database search* problem central to Eddy's work. This method novelly combines the scalable profile HMM search strategy with full topological uncertainty, moving beyond the fixed-tree constraint of Rfam and Pfam.

proposals.title: Phylo-HMMs with Position-Dependent Rate Variation: Modeling Non-Identical Sequence Evolution Across a Phylogeny
proposals.motivation: Standard phylogenetic models, including those used in profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) for homology search, assume sites evolve independently and identically (i.i.d.) under a common substitution process. This assumption is biologically unrealistic, as evolutionary constraints vary dramatically across a protein or RNA sequence due to structure, function, and epistasis. Ignoring this site-specific variation can bias tree inference, ancestral reconstruction, and the detection of homology, particularly for fast-evolving or structurally heterogeneous families.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling sequence positions as non-identically distributed—each with its own empirically determined equilibrium character frequencies and substitution rate matrix—will yield more accurate phylogenetic trees, alignments, and homology detection for diverse protein and RNA families compared to standard i.i.d. models.
proposals.method: We will extend the Phylo-HMM framework to incorporate per-position, lineage-aware substitution models. Starting from a trusted multiple alignment and tree for a gene family (e.g., from Rfam or Pfam), we will use an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to infer a maximum-likelihood general time-reversible (GTR) substitution model for each alignment column, conditioned on the phylogeny. This model of 'position-dependent rate variation' (PDRV) will then be integrated into a new HMM architecture for sensitive homology search, where each match state emits characters according to its inferred PDRV model along the tree, rather than a single i.i.d. emission distribution. We will benchmark against standard profile HMMs (HMMER) and codon-aware models using established test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more powerful probabilistic framework for sequence evolution that directly addresses biological reality. It will deliver a practical software tool for improved homology detection in challenging cases, such as rapidly evolving viral proteins or structured non-coding RNAs with variable regions. The method will also produce interpretable maps of evolutionary constraint across sequence alignments.
proposals.novelty: While site-specific rate heterogeneity is often modeled with a single rate scalar, we propose inferring full, position-specific substitution matrices, integrating this directly into a phylogenetic HMM for search. This moves beyond the i.i.d. assumption at the core of most probabilistic sequence models without abandoning a rigorous statistical framework.

proposals.title: Inferring Overlapping Genetic Codes and Frameshifts with a Multi-Frame Profile HMM
proposals.motivation: Genomic complexity often involves overlapping genes and non-canonical translation events like programmed ribosomal frameshifts (PRFs), which are crucial for viral replication and gene regulation. Current homology search tools (e.g., HMMER, Rfam) and genetic code predictors (e.g., Codetta) typically assume a single, contiguous reading frame per sequence region, limiting their ability to detect and characterize functional elements that occupy overlapping frames or utilize multiple codes.
proposals.hypothesis: A probabilistic model that explicitly represents overlapping reading frames and alternative genetic codes within a single sequence region will significantly improve the sensitivity and specificity of detecting overlapping genes, frameshifts, and other multi-frame coding elements in genomic sequences.
proposals.method: We will extend the profile hidden Markov model (HMM) architecture to a multi-frame, multi-code framework. The model will consist of parallel, interconnected chains of match/states, each representing a distinct reading frame and/or genetic code (e.g., standard, mitochondrial, viral). Transition probabilities between states in different frames will model frameshift events (as in ORFeus), while emission probabilities will be informed by codon adaptation for each specified code (as in Codetta). Parameter estimation will use an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm on curated alignments of known overlapping genes and PRF sites, and search will be implemented via a modified Viterbi algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified probabilistic framework for homology search that accounts for overlapping frames and variable genetic codes. It will yield a new, sensitive search tool for discovering novel overlapping genes and recoding events across diverse genomes, particularly viruses and organelles, and will generate more accurate evolutionary models for these complex regions.
proposals.novelty: While PRF detection (ORFeus) and code prediction (Codetta) exist, no method integrates them into a generalizable homology search model capable of discovering *unexpected* overlaps and code variations de novo, moving beyond the annotation of known cases.

proposals.title: SCFGs for Protein Fold Grammar: A Probabilistic Model for Tertiary Structure Prediction from Sequence
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in homology modeling and deep learning (e.g., AlphaFold2), a rigorous, interpretable probabilistic model for *ab initio* protein structure prediction from sequence remains an open challenge. Stochastic context-free grammars (SCFGs) have been highly successful in modeling RNA secondary and tertiary structure, providing a formal framework for describing long-range dependencies. Applying this powerful, evolutionarily-informed formalism to proteins could yield a new class of models that explicitly capture the hierarchical 'grammar' of protein folding.
proposals.hypothesis: The hierarchical organization of protein tertiary structure—from local secondary structure elements to their spatial assembly into domains—can be effectively modeled by a stochastic context-free grammar, enabling probabilistic inference of 3D structure directly from amino acid sequence.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel SCFG architecture where terminal symbols correspond to structural 'words' (e.g., vectors describing Cα positions and orientations), and productions model the recursive combination of these into motifs, then into larger structural units. The model will be trained on a curated, non-redundant set of protein domains from the PDB, using the Inside-Outside algorithm for parameter estimation. Structure prediction will be performed via the CYK algorithm, extended to output a probabilistic ensemble of parse trees, which can be mapped to 3D coordinate traces. Initial focus will be on single-domain, mainly alpha-helical proteins to establish proof-of-concept.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first fully probabilistic, generative SCFG for protein tertiary structure, providing a rigorous alternative to current black-box predictors. It will deliver not just a single prediction but a probability distribution over possible folds, enabling quantitative uncertainty estimation. The interpretable grammar rules may reveal conserved structural 'phrases' across protein families, offering new insights into folding principles.
proposals.novelty: While SCFGs are a mature technology for RNA, their direct application to model full protein tertiary structure—beyond secondary structure prediction or contact maps—is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from deep learning by providing a transparent, rule-based probabilistic model grounded in evolutionary sequence analysis, aligning with Eddy's expertise in homology and computational grammars.

proposals.title: HMM-Assembler: De novo genome assembly from metagenomes using profile hidden Markov models
proposals.motivation: Current metagenomic assembly pipelines rely on generic de Bruijn graph assemblers, which struggle with strain variation, repetitive elements, and low-abundance species, leading to fragmented genomes. A model-based approach that leverages the deep evolutionary signal of protein families could resolve these ambiguities, improving the recovery of complete genomes from complex environmental samples.
