proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) of conserved protein families as a probabilistic guide during contig extension and binning will produce more complete and accurate metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) than sequence composition or coverage-based methods alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will generate an initial assembly graph using a short-read assembler. Second, we will implement a novel graph traversal algorithm where contig extension decisions are informed by the probabilistic alignment scores of translated reads to a curated set of HMMs from Pfam and TIGRFAM. This HMM-guided walker will prioritize paths that maintain coherent protein family signatures, effectively using homology as an assembly constraint. The method will be integrated into the existing HMMER suite for scalability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, open-source assembly tool that produces higher-quality MAGs, particularly for novel microbial lineages lacking reference genomes. It will also provide a formal framework for incorporating biological domain knowledge (protein families) directly into the core assembly process, moving beyond purely statistical graph algorithms.
proposals.novelty: While HMMs are standard for annotation and gene finding, their direct application to guide the fundamental graph traversal step of de novo metagenomic assembly is a novel synthesis of homology search and genome reconstruction techniques.

proposals.title: SCANR: Single-Cell Alignment and Noise-Robust models for microbial lineage tracing
proposals.motivation: Single-cell genomics reveals the genetic diversity and functional potential of uncultivated microorganisms, but analysis is severely hampered by extreme data sparsity, high error rates, and fragmented assemblies. Pre-2026 methods, designed for bulk sequencing, fail to accurately identify genes or infer evolutionary relationships from these noisy, incomplete single-cell amplified genomes (SAGs). This limits our ability to trace microbial lineages and understand gene content evolution at the most fundamental, cellular level.
proposals.hypothesis: Probabilistic models that explicitly account for the unique noise characteristics and coverage gaps of single-cell sequencing data will enable accurate gene family assignment, homology detection, and phylogenetic inference from SAGs where current methods fail.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage probabilistic framework building on the researcher's expertise in profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) and non-canonical translation. First, we will create 'sparsity-aware' profile HMMs for gene families, using techniques from error-correcting codes to handle missing data and high substitution error rates. Second, we will adapt the logic of Codetta and ORFeus to model lineage-specific genetic code variations and frameshifts as latent variables within a phylogenetic tree, using an expectation-maximization algorithm to jointly infer the tree and these non-canonical events from sparse data.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first dedicated computational toolkit for robust gene family analysis and phylogenetics of single-cell microbial genomes. It will enable more accurate reconstructions of microbial 'dark matter' lineages and allow systematic study of genetic code evolution and programmed frameshifts in uncultivated organisms.
proposals.novelty: While profile HMMs are standard for homology search, no pre-2026 method explicitly models the combined effects of amplification bias, sequencing errors, and massive data dropout inherent to SAGs within a unified probabilistic framework for lineage analysis.

proposals.title: A Whole-Genome Phylogenetic Model Integrating Recombination and Rearrangement
proposals.motivation: Current phylogenetic models are largely limited to single genes or concatenated alignments, which fail to capture the complex evolutionary history of whole genomes shaped by recombination, gene conversion, and structural rearrangements. This limits our ability to infer accurate species trees, understand horizontal gene transfer, and trace the evolution of genomic architecture. A model that scales evolutionary inference to the whole-genome level is a critical unmet need in computational biology.
proposals.hypothesis: A phylogenetic model that explicitly accounts for site-specific histories due to recombination and large-scale rearrangements can be scaled computationally to whole genomes, yielding a more accurate and complete picture of evolutionary relationships than single-gene or concatenated approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a composite likelihood framework that partitions the genome into locally coherent blocks using a hidden Markov model (HMM) for change points, informed by alignment discontinuity and substitution pattern shifts. Phylogenetic trees will be inferred for each block using codon models (extending concepts from Codetta) and profile HMMs (leveraging Rfam/alignment expertise). A hierarchical model will then integrate these block-specific trees, using a graph-based representation to model recombination and rearrangement events between them, with inference via Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical computational framework for generating whole-genome phylogenies that are not misled by recombination. It will produce new software for the community and offer novel insights into the frequency and impact of non-vertical evolutionary events across diverse lineages.
proposals.novelty: While existing methods patch together local gene trees, this proposal integrates the inference of recombination breakpoints, block-specific phylogeny, and genome rearrangement into a single statistical model scaled for genome-wide data, moving beyond treating incongruence as noise to modeling it as a central evolutionary process.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Non-Canonical tRNA Structures in Genomic Sequence
proposals.motivation: Transfer RNA genes are universally defined by a canonical cloverleaf secondary structure, a paradigm enforced by all major detection tools like tRNAscan-SE and ARAGORN. This structural dogma may obscure a hidden diversity of functional tRNAs, particularly in understudied lineages or in contexts like organellar genomes or viral sequences, where structural innovation could enable novel biological functions or regulatory mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functional tRNA genes exist which deviate significantly from the standard cloverleaf model, and that these can be discovered through a homology search strategy that relaxes stringent structural constraints.
proposals.method: We will adapt the profile hidden Markov model (HMM) framework, central to tools like Infernal and the Rfam database, to create a sensitive search model. Instead of a single, strict cloverleaf model, we will construct a series of 'relaxed' HMMs that systematically allow for the absence or drastic rearrangement of one of the four canonical arms (acceptor, D, anticodon, TψC). These models will be used to search a comprehensive set of genomic and metagenomic sequences, with candidate hits rigorously validated by co-variation analysis and, where possible, association with aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase genes or codon usage patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic catalog of non-canonical tRNA genes, challenging a fundamental assumption in molecular biology. It will expand the known structural repertoire of functional RNAs and may reveal new mechanisms of translation or gene regulation in specific viral or microbial lineages.
proposals.novelty: While existing tools are optimized to find canonical tRNAs, this proposal inverts the problem to specifically seek structural outliers, leveraging Eddy lab expertise in sensitive sequence homology (Infernal) and genetic code analysis (Codetta) to validate function beyond mere structural prediction.

proposals.title: tRNAscan-MG: a profile-based search tool for fragmented tRNA gene detection in metagenomic assemblies and long-read sequences
proposals.motivation: The tRNAscan-SE algorithm, a cornerstone for tRNA gene annotation in complete genomes, relies on scanning long, contiguous sequences for characteristic secondary structure. This approach fails on fragmented metagenomic assemblies and raw long-read sequencing data, which are increasingly common. Consequently, tRNA diversity in environmental and uncultured microbial communities remains under-sampled, limiting studies of horizontal gene transfer, codon usage, and microbial evolution.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the covariance models and search strategies from tools like Infernal and Rfam to the specific problem of tRNA detection, we can create a method that accurately identifies tRNA genes in short, fragmented sequences without requiring full-length secondary structure prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a new search pipeline that uses profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) derived from tRNA sequence alignments for initial, fast scanning of fragmented contigs and reads. High-scoring hits will be passed to a streamlined, fragment-aware version of the tRNA covariance model for confirmation, using a banded alignment algorithm to handle sequence ends. The method will be integrated into a Nextflow workflow for scalable processing of large metagenomic datasets, with parameters optimized for common assembly and long-read error profiles.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first dedicated, efficient tool for comprehensive tRNA gene discovery in metagenomic data, expanding the catalog of known tRNAs from uncultured microbes. It will enable new analyses of genetic code variation and tRNA-based phylogeny across diverse environmental samples, directly supporting community efforts like the Earth Microbiome Project.
proposals.novelty: While homology search tools exist, none are specifically optimized for the fragmented tRNA detection problem by combining fast HMM pre-filters with a truncated, probabilistic structural model that does not require full-length sequence context, a core innovation over the monolithic tRNAscan-SE approach.

proposals.title: tRNAscan-BLAST: A rapid, homology-informed pipeline for high-throughput tRNA identification in genomic sequences
proposals.motivation: Accurate tRNA gene annotation is fundamental for understanding the translational capacity and evolutionary history of genomes. While covariance models (CMs) like those in tRNAscan-SE are highly accurate, they are computationally expensive for large-scale or metagenomic surveys. Fast homology search tools like BLAST are efficient but lack the specificity for reliable tRNA detection on their own.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid pipeline that uses a curated, non-redundant database of known tRNAs with BLAST for rapid initial screening, followed by targeted, high-specificity CM verification, will achieve a significant speed increase over whole-genome CM scanning without sacrificing annotation accuracy.
proposals.method: We will first construct a comprehensive, lineage-specific tRNA database by clustering sequences from sources like GtRNAdb and Rfam. This database will be indexed for use with BLASTn and DIAMOND. The proposed pipeline will run a sensitive BLAST search against this index on input genomic sequences. Only genomic regions with significant BLAST hits will be passed to tRNAscan-SE for precise CM alignment and secondary structure verification. We will benchmark this pipeline against standard tRNAscan-SE on diverse eukaryotic, bacterial, and archaeal genomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a publicly available, open-source tool that dramatically accelerates tRNA annotation in large genomic datasets, making systematic comparative tRNA genomics more feasible. It will also yield a high-quality, curated tRNA sequence database indexed for rapid homology search, a resource useful for other studies of non-canonical tRNAs or horizontal gene transfer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the individual components but in their specific integration and optimization for tRNA gene finding. While BLAST is commonly used for gene finding, its application as a pre-filter to strategically constrain the search space for computationally intensive CMs in tRNA annotation is a non-obvious and unexplored efficiency strategy.

proposals.title: Pan-tRNA: A systematic analysis of tRNA gene repertoire variation across bacterial pangenomes
proposals.motivation: Transfer RNA (tRNA) gene complements are fundamental to translation efficiency and can reflect a genome's adaptation to its ecological niche and codon usage biases. While individual tRNA genes are well-annotated, systematic comparisons of tRNA repertoires across thousands of genomes within a species' pangenome are lacking. This gap limits our understanding of how tRNA gene variation contributes to intra-species phenotypic diversity and evolutionary trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tRNA gene repertoires are not static within a species but exhibit structured, lineage-specific variation that correlates with ecological and metabolic traits, and that this variation can be used to infer fine-scale population structure.
proposals.method: We will scale the profile hidden Markov model (HMM) search methodology of Rfam and Infernal to perform sensitive, high-throughput identification of tRNA genes across thousands of genomes from species with established pangenomes (e.g., *E. coli*, *S. pneumoniae*). Building on the Codetta framework for code prediction, we will develop a pipeline to classify tRNA isotypes and anticodons, cluster genes into orthologous families, and quantify presence/absence and copy number variation. We will integrate this with pangenome annotations to correlate tRNA variants with accessory gene content and use phylogenetic comparative methods to map tRNA repertoire evolution onto lineage trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive atlas of tRNA repertoire variation at the pangenome scale, providing a public resource of tRNA gene families. It will establish methods to quantify this variation and reveal its links to genome evolution and adaptation, moving beyond single-reference thinking to a population-genetic view of a core translational component.
proposals.novelty: While tRNA prediction in single genomes is routine, applying pangenome-scale comparative genomics specifically to tRNA gene complements is novel. The approach moves from cataloging genes in isolation to analyzing them as variable, heritable traits across entire populations.

proposals.title: A Network Model of ncRNA Evolution: From Discrete Families to a Continuum of Homology
proposals.motivation: Current databases like Rfam model non-coding RNA (ncRNA) evolution as a set of discrete, non-overlapping families. This discrete model is a simplification that may obscure complex evolutionary relationships, such as chimeric RNAs, deep ancestral connections between families, and the continuum of sequence divergence. A more nuanced model is needed to better represent the full complexity of ncRNA evolution and improve homology detection at the margins of existing families.
proposals.hypothesis: ncRNA homology relationships are better represented as a weighted network or continuum than as a partition into discrete families, revealing previously obscured evolutionary connections and improving the sensitivity of remote homology search.
proposals.method: We will develop a method to construct a network model of ncRNA homology. Starting from all Rfam families, we will use Infernal's covariance models for all-vs-all sensitive searches. Pairwise similarity scores, incorporating both sequence and structural alignment scores, will be used to build a weighted graph. We will then apply graph clustering algorithms (e.g., Markov Clustering, affinity propagation) that allow for overlapping clusters and soft boundaries to identify core families and inter-family connections. This will be implemented as an extension to the existing Rfam pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a public, navigable network resource of ncRNA homology, extending the Rfam paradigm. It will generate specific, testable predictions about evolutionary relationships between established families and provide a more sensitive framework for classifying new sequences that fall between existing family boundaries.
proposals.novelty: While network approaches exist for protein families, applying this to the structured ncRNA domain, where Rfam's discrete model is the entrenched standard, is novel. The key innovation is moving from a partition to a continuum model explicitly for ncRNA, leveraging the Eddy lab's deep expertise in covariance models and RNA family analysis.

proposals.title: Context-first discovery and model building for lineage-specific non-coding RNAs
proposals.motivation: Current ncRNA discovery, as in Rfam, relies on building covariance models from pre-aligned, curated sequence families, biasing databases toward conserved, well-characterized RNAs. This approach systematically misses lineage-specific ncRNAs that lack clear homology across species. A method that starts from genomic and transcriptomic context within a specific lineage could uncover a hidden layer of functional RNA elements.
proposals.hypothesis: By first identifying candidate ncRNA loci using genomic context features (e.g., promoter motifs, conservation patterns, synteny) and expression data (e.g., RNA-seq), we can build accurate probabilistic models de novo for lineage-specific RNA families that are missed by homology-based searches.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline that, for a target lineage, integrates RNA-seq expression signals with genomic features like conserved intergenic regions and predicted transcription factor binding sites to define candidate loci. These loci will be clustered using sequence and secondary structure similarity. For each cluster, we will build a specialized profile model, extending methods from Infernal and Codetta, that incorporates lineage-specific mutation patterns and structural constraints, iteratively refining the model with new sequences identified by the initial search.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a new class of ncRNA family models explicitly tuned for lineage-specific biology, expanding the catalog of functional RNAs. The method will be implemented as open-source software and validated by discovering novel ncRNA candidates in understudied eukaryotic lineages, providing a complementary approach to Rfam.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard model-building paradigm by using contextual evidence to define the sequence set before model construction, rather than requiring a pre-aligned family. It directly targets the discovery of RNAs with limited phylogenetic distribution, a known blind spot in existing databases.

proposals.title: A Unified, Phylogenetically-Informed Nomenclature for Non-Coding RNA Gene Families
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of non-coding RNA (ncRNA) families in databases like Rfam has led to inconsistent and ambiguous naming conventions, hindering comparative genomics, literature searches, and database interoperability. A standardized system is urgently needed to resolve conflicts where the same family has multiple names (e.g., Y RNA, Ro RNA) or where similar names denote distinct families, creating confusion in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic nomenclature for ncRNA families, based on phylogenetic lineage and conserved sequence/structure features, can be algorithmically applied to resolve current inconsistencies and provide a stable, scalable naming framework for newly discovered families.
proposals.method: We will develop a rule-based algorithm that ingests Rfam alignments and phylogenetic trees to assign unique, informative names. The method will first cluster families by sequence homology and structural similarity using Infernal and CD-HIT. It will then apply a naming grammar incorporating taxonomic lineage (e.g., 'Bacteria', 'Eukaryota'), conserved structural domains (e.g., 'HDV_ribozyme'), and, where applicable, functional descriptors from Gene Ontology. Disambiguation of existing names will be achieved through an independent set algorithm to select a single canonical name per cluster, referencing literature precedence.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly accessible, version-controlled nomenclature map for all Rfam families and a software tool for naming new families. It will directly improve the consistency of ncRNA annotation in genomic databases and provide a critical resource for tool developers and curators, facilitating clearer communication in the RNA community.
proposals.novelty: While naming conventions exist for proteins, a comprehensive, algorithmic system for ncRNAs that integrates phylogenetic lineage with structural classification to actively resolve legacy conflicts is novel. This moves beyond simple curation to provide an enforceable, logic-driven standard.

proposals.title: DeepMOTIF: Detecting Conserved Sequence Patterns in Multiple Alignments Using Convolutional Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Identifying conserved motifs in multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) is fundamental for inferring homology, function, and structure. Current profile-based methods (e.g., HMMs) and motif-finders can struggle with complex, non-linear patterns of conservation, especially those involving spatially distributed residues or subtle covariation signals. A new approach is needed to capture higher-order patterns that are visually apparent to experts but difficult to encode in traditional probabilistic models.
proposals.hypothesis: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs), trained on labeled MSAs, can learn to recognize complex, spatially extended motifs and patterns of conservation and variation more sensitively and specifically than current position-independent or pairwise co-variance methods.
proposals.method: We will treat MSAs as 2D matrices (sequences x positions) with residues encoded as one-hot vectors, creating image-like tensors. We will train a CNN architecture (e.g., ResNet-18) on a curated benchmark of RFAM alignments, using known family annotations as labels. The network will be trained to classify alignments into families and to output saliency maps highlighting conserved regions. We will compare its motif detection performance against HMMER and Infernal on held-out families and on simulated alignments with embedded complex motifs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, open-source tool for sensitive motif discovery in MSAs, potentially revealing novel non-canonical functional elements missed by current methods. It will also provide a framework for visualizing and interpreting the 'features' learned by the network, offering new insights into the nature of sequence conservation.
proposals.novelty: While CNNs are established in computer vision, their direct application to raw MSA matrices for unsupervised or supervised pattern discovery is largely unexplored. This approach bypasses the need for hand-crafted statistical summaries (like profiles) and allows the model to learn directly from the alignment 'image'.

proposals.title: Network-Based Clustering of Non-Coding RNA Homology Graphs for Family Delineation
proposals.motivation: Accurate clustering of non-coding RNA (ncRNA) sequences into homologous families is fundamental for annotation, evolutionary analysis, and functional inference. Current methods, like those used in Rfam, rely heavily on sequence alignment and covariance models, which can struggle with highly divergent sequences or those with complex structural homology where primary sequence similarity is low. A more robust, alignment-free or alignment-informed clustering method could improve the sensitivity and accuracy of ncRNA family discovery, especially in large-scale metagenomic surveys.
proposals.hypothesis: Community detection algorithms from social network analysis, when applied to graphs of ncRNA sequence similarity, can identify homologous families with greater sensitivity and specificity than traditional hierarchical clustering or fixed-threshold methods, particularly for distantly related sequences.
proposals.method: We will construct weighted homology graphs from ncRNA sequences, where nodes are sequences and edges are weighted by similarity scores from tools like Infernal or BLASTN. We will then apply and evaluate several network community detection algorithms (e.g., the Louvain method, Infomap, and label propagation) to partition the graph into clusters representing putative families. The pipeline will be benchmarked against the manually curated Rfam database and existing automated clustering methods, using metrics of cluster purity, recall of known families, and the biological coherence of novel clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available software tool for ncRNA family discovery that complements and extends the Rfam pipeline. It will yield a more principled, automated framework for delineating families from sequence similarity data, potentially identifying novel ncRNA families missed by current methods and refining the boundaries of existing ones.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis is used in protein family prediction, its systematic application to the specific challenges of ncRNA homology—where structural constraints create distinct patterns of sequence conservation—is novel. This approach moves beyond simple similarity thresholds to leverage the global topology of the homology network.

proposals.title: R-Tree Indexing of Sequence Composition for Accelerated MSV Filtering in Protein Homology Search
proposals.motivation: The MSV (Multiple Segment Viterbi) filter in profile HMM search tools like HMMER is a critical first stage for speed, but it still performs a linear scan over all database sequences. As sequence databases grow exponentially, this linear scan becomes a bottleneck. A method to pre-index the database for rapid compositional pruning would provide a fundamental acceleration to the entire homology search pipeline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sequence composition of protein profiles or their constituent k-mers can be effectively mapped to a multi-dimensional feature space where spatial proximity correlates with MSV score potential, enabling the use of R-tree spatial indexing to prune distant database entries before alignment.
proposals.method: We will represent each database sequence profile by a feature vector, such as its di-nucleotide or amino acid k-mer frequency, or a low-dimensional embedding from an autoencoder. These vectors will be indexed using an R-tree or an R*-tree, which hierarchically groups sequences with similar composition. For a query, its feature vector is computed, and the R-tree is queried for the top-k nearest neighbors or sequences within a bounded distance. Only this candidate set proceeds to the full, costly MSV alignment calculation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source acceleration layer for HMMER's MSV stage, significantly reducing search time for large databases. It will also provide a proof-of-concept for applying robust geospatial data structures to sequence analysis, a cross-disciplinary methodological advance.
proposals.novelty: While k-mer indexing is common in BLAST, applying hierarchical spatial indexing (R-trees) to profile-based search and using it to accelerate the specific, alignment-based MSV filter is a novel adaptation of a GIS technique to biological sequence analysis.

proposals.title: Accelerating HMMER's MSV Filter with BLAST-Style Database Indexing
proposals.motivation: The HMMER software suite is a cornerstone for sensitive homology search of protein and nucleotide sequences, but its speed remains a bottleneck for large-scale analyses. The initial MSV (Multiple Segment Viterbi) filter is computationally expensive, scanning every position in a sequence database. As sequence databases grow exponentially, accelerating this first filter stage would significantly improve the throughput of searches for novel gene families and non-canonical translation events, which are central to Eddy lab research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying sequence database indexing techniques, analogous to those used in BLAST (e.g., seed-and-extend with a lookup table of k-mers), can dramatically accelerate the MSV filter stage without compromising its sensitivity for downstream HMMER stages.
proposals.method: We will adapt the core principle of BLAST's database indexing: pre-computing a lookup table of all k-mers (or spaced seeds) from the target sequence database. The MSV filter, which uses a profile HMM's consensus sequence, will first query this index to identify potential hit locations, skipping large swaths of the database. We will implement this within the HMMER codebase, optimizing parameters like k-mer length and index memory footprint. The performance and sensitivity of the indexed MSV filter will be benchmarked against the standard HMMER3 scan on standard protein family test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a substantially faster initial scan for HMMER, making large-scale, iterative searches more practical. It will directly benefit projects like Codetta and ORFeus that rely on scanning massive genomic datasets. The integration of a BLAST-inspired heuristic into a profile HMM search pipeline represents a novel hybrid acceleration strategy for the community.
proposals.novelty: While database indexing is standard in BLAST, its application to accelerate the specific, profile HMM-based MSV filter in HMMER is novel. The key challenge is designing an index compatible with the MSV's profile consensus logic, not a simple amino acid identity match.

proposals.title: StreamHMM: Online, Adaptive Synthesis of Sequences from a Streaming Profile Hidden Markov Model
proposals.motivation: Current profile HMM methods, like those in HMMER and Rfam, are batch-oriented, requiring a fixed, pre-collected dataset for model construction and sequence generation. This is ill-suited for modern, high-throughput biological data streams (e.g., from real-time sequencing or continuous environmental sampling) where underlying sequence families may evolve or drift. An online system that both learns from and generates sequences in real-time would enable adaptive experimental design and dynamic hypothesis testing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming profile HMM, updated via online Expectation-Maximization (EM) variants, can be coupled with an efficient inverse sampling algorithm to synthesize biologically plausible sequences that accurately reflect the model's current statistical profile as it adapts to new data.
proposals.method: We will develop a core streaming HMM architecture using incremental Baum-Welch or online variational Bayes updates for model parameters. For synthesis, we will implement a stochastic traceback algorithm that samples a path through the current model and emits a sequence, operating on the same incremental data structures. The system will be implemented in C/C++ with a Python interface, building on the HMMER codebase, and evaluated on simulated drifting sequence families and real-time metagenomic data streams.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source framework for online, coupled analysis and generation of biological sequences from a streaming statistical model. It will extend the utility of profile HMMs from static database search to active, real-time applications in synthetic biology and surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While online learning for HMMs exists, the tight integration of real-time, model-driven sequence synthesis to create a closed-loop, adaptive system is novel. This moves beyond passive analysis to active, continuous generation guided by an evolving model.

proposals.title: hmmer3-lite: A Profile HMM Pipeline Optimized for Real-Time Taxonomic Classification of Short-Read Metagenomic Data
proposals.motivation: Real-time analysis of sequencing data, as from Oxford Nanopore or Illumina platforms, is crucial for applications like pathogen detection and microbiome monitoring. Current profile HMM tools like HMMER3, while highly sensitive for homology search, are designed for deep, accurate analysis of full-length sequences and are too computationally intensive for low-latency classification of short reads. This creates a gap where the specificity of curated protein family models (e.g., from Pfam/Rfam) is unavailable for real-time pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically simplifying the HMMER3 pipeline's alignment and scoring stages and implementing a novel, lightweight pre-filter, we can achieve taxonomic classification accuracy comparable to standard HMMER3 for short reads while reducing latency by over an order of magnitude, enabling real-time use.
proposals.method: We will develop a streamlined pipeline, hmmer3-lite, that modifies HMMER3 for short-read input. First, we will replace the MSV and bias filters with a novel, learned k-mer bloom filter trained on target family sequences to rapidly eliminate non-homologous reads. Second, we will optimize the Viterbi and Forward scoring algorithms for very short sequences by pre-computing and caching emission score matrices for fixed-length windows. Finally, we will implement a just-in-time model loading system to keep only active taxonomic lineage-specific HMMs in memory, based on a real-time taxonomic prior from a fast nucleotide k-mer classifier.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, open-source tool that brings the sensitivity and specificity of profile HMM searches to real-time metagenomic analysis. It will provide a robust method for precise, gene-centric taxonomic assignment of streaming short reads, directly interfacing with sequencer output. This will expand the utility of curated protein family databases for dynamic, time-sensitive biological questions.
proposals.novelty: While other tools accelerate HMMER3 via hardware (e.g., GPUs), our approach is novel in its algorithmic co-design for the specific constraints of short reads and low-latency execution, introducing a learned pre-filter and cache-aware optimizations not present in the standard pipeline. It uniquely focuses on integrating real-time taxonomic priors to manage the model search space.

proposals.title: Compressive Modeling of Sequence Databases: A Profile HMM Library as a Lossy, Searchable Archive
proposals.motivation: The exponential growth of public sequence databases creates immense storage and distribution burdens. Traditional compression focuses on bit-level efficiency, discarding biological semantics. A method that compresses sequence data into a library of interpretable, reusable probabilistic models would offer a biologically meaningful, lossy compression scheme, enabling compact archiving and direct knowledge reuse for homology search and annotation.
proposals.hypothesis: A database of protein or RNA sequences can be efficiently and lossily compressed into a library of profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) such that the original sequences can be approximately reconstructed from the models, and the model library itself serves as a powerful, compact tool for sequence analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop an iterative algorithm that clusters sequences, builds a profile HMM for each cluster using HMMER3, and then uses the HMM to 'encode' member sequences by their Viterbi paths. Sequences poorly encoded by existing models seed new clusters. The process iterates until a compression ratio threshold is met. We will adapt independent set algorithms, as used in benchmark construction, to select a non-redundant set of seed sequences for initial clustering, ensuring model diversity and efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for semantic compression of biological sequence data, yielding a reusable model library. It will provide a concrete method for distilling massive databases into a manageable set of generative models, facilitating faster database distribution and novel analyses of model space itself.
proposals.novelty: While profile HMMs are used for search and family definition, their systematic application as a lossy compression codec for entire databases is unexplored. The inverse perspective—treating the model as the compressed object rather than the search tool—represents a significant conceptual shift from standard practice in computational biology.

ideas.title: Online, Adaptive Synthesis of Sequences Matching a Streaming Profile HMM
ideas.description: This research direction develops streaming algorithms that not only analyze an infinite sequence data stream to build and update a profile HMM online but also immediately use that evolving model to synthesize new sequences that match its current statistical profile. The system operates in a tight feedback loop: as new data arrives, the model is updated incrementally, and the inverse generation component produces candidate sequences that reflect the latest learned constraints and patterns. This enables real-time, adaptive generation of plausible sequences (e.g., protein variants) that track drifting concepts or emerging patterns in the stream, which is impossible with static batch processing. Potential applications include generating real-time decoys or probes for security monitoring, creating adaptive training sets for machine learning, or driving continuous experimental design in synthetic biology.

ideas.title: Federated Adversarial Sequence Generation and Benchmarking for Profile HMMs
ideas.description: This project develops a federated platform where a central generator creates synthetic protein sequences designed to score highly against a target profile HMM. These candidate sequences are then broadcast to a distributed network of institutional HMMER servers, each hosting distinct, potentially private, HMM databases. The aggregated search results provide a comprehensive fitness landscape, revealing if the synthetic sequences are uniquely specific to the target or if they also match unrelated families. This feedback loop allows for adversarial refinement of the generator to produce sequences with high specificity, creating powerful benchmarks for HMM sensitivity/selectivity and potentially uncovering subtle biases or limitations in widely used profile models.

ideas.title: Query-Aware, Multi-Relational Network Ranking for Biologically Contextual Sequence Search
ideas.description: This system moves beyond applying a generic web-ranking algorithm by explicitly modeling the biological semantics of sequence relationships. It constructs a multi-layer network from database hits where different edge types represent distinct biological relationships derived from the similarity data. A machine learning model, conditioned on the user's query intent and trained on gold-standard benchmarks, dynamically fuses evidence from these network layers with traditional alignment scores. This results in a final ranking that prioritizes hits based on their inferred biological relevance to the specific search context, not just their topological centrality in a simplistic graph.

ideas.title: Constrained Adversarial HMM Optimization for Specificity Engineering
ideas.description: This method performs a constrained search for profile HMMs that are explicitly tuned to avoid matching a given negative sequence while remaining high-fidelity models for a desired family or property. The core is a multi-term loss function that penalizes high probability for the negative sequence, rewards high likelihood for positive training sequences, and maintains the model within a plausible, learnable parameter space. This enables the 'negative design' of discriminative models for tasks like avoiding off-target binding or engineering protein domains that exclude certain structural motifs, producing HMMs that are both informative and purposefully selective.

ideas.title: Online, Context-Aware Ranking for Streaming Sequence Search
ideas.description: This research fuses the principles of web search ranking with streaming algorithms to create a novel system for processing infinite streams of biological sequence data. It constructs and continuously updates a dynamic sequence similarity network in an online fashion, applying incremental, PageRank-like algorithms to assign real-time importance scores to each sequence match. This allows the system to prioritize the most contextually significant hits from the stream for immediate analysis or storage, rather than treating all matches equally. The result is a scalable pipeline for applications like real-time pathogen surveillance or metagenomic classification that intelligently filters and ranks streaming data based on evolving network centrality.

ideas.title: Ranking biological sequence hits with bibliometric citation network algorithms
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from bibliometrics and scientometrics, where the importance of a scholarly article is determined by its position in a citation network (e.g., through co-citation analysis), not just its raw citation count. The analogous solution involves analyzing networks of citations to identify central, influential papers based on shared citation patterns. Adapted to bioinformatics, we apply this to sequence similarity search results (e.g., from BLAST). We construct a network where nodes are database hits and edges represent significant sequence similarity. We then employ algorithms analogous to bibliographic coupling or co-citation—here, 'co-similarity' analysis—to rank hits. A hit is considered more important if it is similar to many other hits that are also similar to each other, indicating it is part of a central, densely connected cluster in the similarity network. This moves beyond simple E-values or bit scores to rank database hits by their contextual importance within the entire set of results, prioritizing sequences that are core members of conserved protein families or functional groups.

ideas.title: Applying Geospatial R-Tree Indexing to MSV Filter Acceleration
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Geographic Information Systems (GIS) to accelerate the MSV scan stage in protein sequence search. In GIS, R-tree data structures are used to efficiently index multi-dimensional spatial objects (like points and rectangles) by grouping nearby objects and representing them with minimum bounding rectangles in a hierarchical tree, enabling fast spatial queries. We propose to adapt this concept by using R-trees or similar spatial access methods to index the sequence feature space of the database—such as k-mer frequency vectors or low-dimensional embeddings of sequence profiles. The hierarchical structure would allow the MSV filter to perform rapid range searches or nearest-neighbor searches in this feature space, pruning entire branches of the database that are compositionally distant from the query, thereby reducing the number of full MSV alignments computed and significantly accelerating the initial filtering stage.

ideas.title: Calibrating a Heuristic Filter Using a Known, Distant Ground Truth
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of 'negligible sensitivity loss' for the MSV filter when applied to extremely divergent homologs by adapting a validation method from astrophysics. In exoplanet detection, the sensitivity of algorithms for extremely faint signals is calibrated using blind injection and recovery tests on real data. We adapt this by creating a benchmark where sequences with known but extremely weak homology signals are artificially embedded into background genomic data, masking their locations. By running the MSV filter on this benchmark, we can precisely quantify its true sensitivity and recovery rate at these distant homology limits, providing empirical evidence to confirm or refute the assumption about its performance degradation.

ideas.description: This system integrates queries against multiple curated profile HMM databases but is specifically engineered to handle the inevitable conflicts and redundancies between them. It uses a consensus engine to synthesize results into a unified, confidence-weighted functional prediction, prioritizing annotations based on source database reliability, score significance, and taxonomic relevance. The web interface presents this clear consensus first, while providing drill-down panels to inspect all raw database outputs and the logic behind any conflict resolution. This approach transforms database multiplicity from a source of confusion into a robust, evidence-rich annotation framework.

ideas.title: Single Optimal Segment Filtering via Signal Processing's Matched Filter Principle
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that multiple ungapped segments are necessary for effective homology search filtering by drawing an analogy from signal processing. In radar detection, the optimal method to find a known signal in noise is a single 'matched filter,' not a suite of weaker filters. Adapting this solution, the research would explore designing a single, optimally shaped alignment segment or scoring profile based on the query's composition and the target database's background statistics. This single segment would act as a coarser but theoretically optimal first-pass filter, maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio for potential homologs. The goal is to achieve faster database scanning by reducing computational complexity, trading some sensitivity for speed in the initial filtering stage, much like how a matched filter provides the fastest, optimal detection point before finer analysis.

ideas.title: Adaptive Indexing for Divergent Homology Search: A Sensitivity-Aware MSV Filter
ideas.description: This research proposes to integrate database indexing into the MSV filter in a novel, query-adaptive manner. Instead of a single, static index optimized for speed, the system would construct or select from multiple index profiles (e.g., varying k-mer sizes or spaced seed patterns) based on a preliminary assessment of query divergence or the desired sensitivity threshold. For highly divergent queries with weak signals, the method would automatically employ a more sensitive, conservative indexing strategy to minimize the risk of filter failure, while for less divergent queries, it would use a maximally aggressive index for speed. This creates a unified framework that directly addresses the trade-off between acceleration and sensitivity, making the indexed MSV filter robust for deep homology searches without sacrificing performance on easier targets.

ideas.title: Hardware-Aware Machine Learning for Adaptive Vectorized Sequence Search
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that co-optimizes the algorithmic heuristics and low-level hardware execution for sequence search. A machine learning model, trained on profiles of query sequences and hardware performance counters, learns to predict the optimal configuration for the striped vector-parallel MSV algorithm. This includes selecting the best available instruction set (e.g., AVX-512 vs. AVX2), tuning architecture-specific parameters like GPU thread block size, and setting the sensitivity threshold dynamically per query. The result is a single adaptive system that maximizes throughput and efficiency across diverse hardware platforms without manual tuning.

The synergy transforms the static 'single vs. multiple' debate into an adaptive, real-time exploration of the optimality frontier.

ideas.title: Adaptive Quotient Filters with Feedback Control for Dynamic Sequence Pre-screening
ideas.description: This design employs a counting quotient filter (CQF) as a dynamic, deletable pre-filter instead of a standard Bloom filter. A feedback loop continuously monitors the load on the subsequent MSV stage, adjusting the filter's occupancy by evicting low-frequency k-mers to maintain a configurable target for unnecessary MSV computations. This adaptive approach ensures optimal trade-off between filter memory footprint and computational savings across varying input datasets and search depths, preventing the fixed trade-off inherent in static probabilistic structures.

ideas.title: Regime-Aware Adaptive Rescaling for Stable HMM Inference in Extreme Probability Landscapes
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical and algorithmic framework to ensure the robustness of Hidden Markov Model (HMM) forward-backward algorithms across all probability regimes, including those dominated by many low-probability states. It begins by deriving formal conditions under which standard sparse rescaling fails, quantifying error accumulation as a function of state-space sparsity and transition structure. Using this theory, the project designs an adaptive rescaling algorithm that monitors the entropy and dynamic range of the state distribution at each time step, automatically switching between dense log-space operations, sparse rescaling, or hybrid methods to preserve numerical precision. The final system is validated through rigorous benchmarks on synthetic and real-world HMMs with pathological probability landscapes, demonstrating guaranteed stability without sacrificing computational efficiency.

ideas.title: Adaptive Reservoir Sampling for Streaming Data via Ecological Succession Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology, specifically models of ecological succession, to the problem of streaming algorithms for infinite data. In ecology, state-and-transition models efficiently predict the probabilistic evolution of an entire ecosystem (e.g., from grassland to forest) without exhaustive historical data. The adapted research idea applies this conceptual framework to design novel streaming algorithms. The algorithm will maintain a dynamic, finite reservoir representing the current 'state' of the data stream. Inspired by succession transitions, the rules for updating this reservoir—selecting, retaining, or discarding elements—will be governed by an online model that reacts to changes in data distribution and velocity, creating an adaptive, memory-bounded summary that evolves meaningfully with the infinite sequence.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Hardware-Aware HMMER for Heterogeneous Mobile and Edge Devices
ideas.description: This research develops a single, unified codebase for a portable HMMER that can run on smartphones, tablets, and edge devices. Its core innovation is an adaptive runtime engine that profiles the device's hardware—detecting available CPU instruction sets (e.g., NEON, AVX2, or AVX-512) and GPU compute capabilities—and then selects and executes a optimally vectorized version of the MSV (Multiple Segment Viterbi) algorithm for that specific platform. This allows the same application to provide desktop-level search speed on high-end mobile devices with advanced SIMD, while still functioning efficiently on low-end devices via simpler vector units or fallback scalar code, all while operating entirely offline on local sequence databases.

ideas.title: Synteny implies functional conservation

ideas.title: Local, Privacy-Preserving Collaborative Filtering for Bioinformatics Search
ideas.description: This research develops a browser-based HMMER client that incorporates a local collaborative filtering model. The system analyzes a user's local interaction history with search results and privately compares it with anonymized, aggregated interaction patterns from a peer community of researchers, which are downloaded as encrypted model updates. It then annotates local search results with indicators of relevance based on what similar researchers found useful, all without transmitting sensitive query or interaction data to a central server. This fusion provides the convenience of community-driven insights while strictly adhering to the principle of local user control and data privacy.

ideas.title: Cardinal Segment Set: A Minimal Ensemble for Robust Homology Filtering
ideas.description: This approach abandons the pursuit of a single optimal segment in favor of constructing a minimal set of cardinal segments that together act as a coarser, faster filter. The system algorithmically selects segments that are maximally informative and non-redundant, ensuring coverage of key conserved regions even when homology is patchy or multi-scale. This design preserves the speed advantage of a small-segment filter while dramatically improving sensitivity and robustness compared to a single segment, effectively mitigating the core failure mode of signal loss.

ideas.title: Adapting Edge Computing for Genomics: A Federated, Browser-Based HMMER Network
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that centralized web services are necessary for convenience by adapting a solution from distributed edge computing. The analogous problem of processing data locally for privacy and efficiency is solved via federated architectures where computation happens at the network edge. We adapt this by developing a browser-based HMMER client ecosystem coordinated by a lightweight server. This server breaks large search jobs into segments and distributes them to participating users' browsers, which run the computations locally using WebAssembly or similar technologies. The local clients return only the processed results (e.g., hits, scores) back to the coordinator for aggregation, never the raw, potentially sensitive query sequences. This creates a convenient web-accessible service that fundamentally operates through local user control, leveraging distributed hardware for scalability while ensuring data privacy and user autonomy.

ideas.title: Federated, Consistent HMM Search via Ephemeral, Version-Controlled Compute Tasks
ideas.description: This system provides scalable, consistent federated HMM searches by avoiding dependence on heterogeneous institutional HMMER servers. A central coordinator manages certified, versioned snapshots of HMM databases and search software as container images. Upon receiving a query, it dynamically packages the search into isolated, ephemeral tasks dispatched to a federated compute pool provided by participating institutions, ensuring identical runtime environments. Results are aggregated using statistical models that account for task completion confidence and scores, providing robust, reproducible outputs independent of local server states or configurations.

ideas.title: Crowdsourced benchmarking of sparse rescaling via a shared user platform
ideas.description: This project develops a platform with user accounts that allows researchers to upload, save, and share their Hidden Markov Model (HMM) analyses and parameter sets. The system automatically logs performance metrics—including accuracy failures or computational anomalies—when sparse rescaling techniques are applied to these models, particularly those with many low-probability states. By aggregating and anonymizing results across users, it builds a large-scale, crowdsourced benchmark dataset. This enables the community to identify precise failure modes and robustness boundaries of rescaling methods, accelerating validation and improvement of numerical algorithms through shared, reproducible experiments.

ideas.title: Implement a Versioned, Collaborative Repository for Search Sessions
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from bioinformatics platforms like Galaxy, which allow scientists to save, version, and share complex data analysis workflows for reproducibility and collaboration, this idea proposes a 'Search Session Repository'. User authentication enables the system to capture not just a search query, but the entire interactive session—including filters, result sets, user-highlighted items, and custom ranking preferences—as a versioned object stored on the server. These saved search sessions become shareable, forkable assets. Users can collaborate by iterating on each other's searches, creating branches for different investigative pathways, and building libraries of proven search workflows for specific research tasks, thereby enhancing reproducibility and collective intelligence within the platform.

ideas.title: Collaborative Evidence Synthesis for Deliberative Search
ideas.description: This research proposes a deliberative search system that forgoes single-query speed to perform deep, multi-source evidence synthesis for complex research questions. The system integrates collaborative filtering not on final results, but on the intermediate evidence fragments and sources, highlighting connections and corroborations previously made by researchers with similar profiles or intents. This allows the system to construct and present a synthesized 'evidence narrative' annotated with community-driven credibility signals and alternative interpretations. Consequently, the search becomes a guided exploration of a knowledge landscape, where slowness is leveraged to build context and collaboration, rather than merely to process information.

ideas.title: A REST API with a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) Workflow Engine
ideas.description: This research expands REST APIs for complex, multi-step workflows by drawing an analogy from bioinformatics pipeline engines like Snakemake. In genomics, complex analyses involving chained tools and intermediate files are solved by a workflow engine that interprets a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) specification, managing execution and state. Adapted to web services, the proposal is to create a REST API with a built-in workflow engine. A client would POST a declarative DAG document to a dedicated endpoint, where nodes define HTTP sub-requests (e.g., searches, CRUD operations, data processing calls) and edges define how outputs map to subsequent inputs. The API engine then orchestrates the remote execution of this graph, managing the lifecycle of intermediate results, enforcing dependencies, handling errors, and providing a unified interface for monitoring and retrieving the final workflow output, thereby enabling complex, chained remote computations within a standard RESTful framework.

ideas.title: Massively parallel search on cloud databases

ideas.title: Adaptive Citation Highlighting via Collaborative Filtering and Social Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from epidemiology's contact tracing and risk network analysis. In public health, solutions analyze interaction patterns within a population network to identify and prioritize high-risk individuals or transmission paths. Adapting this, we model the research community as a social and citation network where researchers are nodes connected by collaboration and interest similarity. A hybrid method combines collaborative filtering with network analysis metrics (e.g., centrality, community detection) to annotate search results. This system would highlight papers or resources that are not only frequently selected by similar users but are also influential or representative within the specific intellectual sub-community (or 'exposure network') of the searcher, providing more contextually relevant and field-specific annotations.

ideas.title: Provably Adaptive Streaming Algorithms for Non-Stationary Infinite Data
ideas.description: This research designs streaming algorithms for sequences of potentially infinite length with formal guarantees under non-stationarity. It models the stream as generated by a process with bounded concept drift and integrates adaptive online learning with change-detection to maintain relevance. The core contribution is a suite of algorithms that dynamically adjust their update rules and memory, providing sublinear regret against a time-varying optimal predictor. This ensures robust, long-term performance without requiring manual intervention or predefined data windows.

ideas.title: A Generative Framework for Portable, Hardware-Aware Vectorization Strategy Synthesis
ideas.description: This research designs a system that automatically synthesizes and evaluates multiple hardware-specific vectorization strategies for computational kernels, moving beyond a single implementation. It uses a portable intermediate representation for data-parallel patterns and a pluggable cost model fed by machine profiles (e.g., for AVX-512, SVE, or GPU SIMT). The framework generates numerous code variants, benchmarks them on real hardware, and analyzes the results to derive predictive principles for mapping algorithm characteristics to optimal vectorization approaches. This creates a robust, forward-looking methodology for performance portability rather than a point-in-time benchmark.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hybrid HMMER: Intelligent workload partitioning for universal usability
ideas.description: This system develops a browser-based HMMER client that dynamically chooses the optimal execution path for each search. It begins all jobs locally to preserve the user's sense of control and initial privacy. Using a lightweight profiler, it estimates runtime and resource demands, then automatically and transparently offloads only the heavy computational stages to a transient cloud function when local execution would be impractical. This ensures searches always complete reliably and in a reasonable time, regardless of user hardware, while maintaining a strict principle of no permanent data retention on remote servers.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware ML for Robust Heuristic Threshold Tuning with Safe Fallbacks
ideas.description: This system uses machine learning to predict optimal heuristic thresholds per query, but crucially incorporates a model to estimate the prediction's epistemic uncertainty. If the uncertainty is below a calibrated risk tolerance, the ML-predicted threshold is applied for optimal performance. If uncertainty is high, indicating a novel or ambiguous query, the system seamlessly switches to a provably safe, conservatively computed threshold based on query features and worst-case analytical bounds. This hybrid approach guarantees robust baseline performance while allowing ML to optimize the speed/sensitivity trade-off only when it can do so reliably.

ideas.title: Adaptive Threshold Tuning via Reinforcement Learning from Feedback Loops
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Process Control Engineering, where adaptive PID controllers dynamically tune their parameters based on real-time system feedback to optimize performance. The analogous problem of static heuristic thresholds in sequence search is solved by implementing a reinforcement learning-based feedback loop. A lightweight RL agent is integrated into the search pipeline; after each query (or batch of queries), it analyzes performance outcomes like execution time and recall/precision metrics. Using this feedback, the agent proactively adjusts the MSV score threshold for future queries, creating a system that continuously self-optimizes the speed/sensitivity trade-off per query or query type without requiring extensive offline pre-training.

ideas.title: Predictive, Constraint-Aware Scheduling for Heterogeneous Batch Query Workloads
ideas.description: This system addresses the core flaw of applying HFT techniques to heterogeneous batch workloads by designing a hybrid scheduler. It employs lightweight online profiling to predict the resource demands (CPU, memory, I/O) of each incoming batch query. These predictions feed a multi-armed bandit-based dispatcher that makes ultra-fast placement decisions, optimizing not just for queue delay but also for preventing node-level resource contention and minimizing tail latency. The result is a robust scheduling layer that maintains high throughput and low latency even under highly variable and demanding query loads.

ideas.title: Progressive Answer Synthesis with Immediate First-Answer and Background Multi-Source Integration
ideas.description: This system addresses the latency problem by providing a useful, provisional answer within a standard fast-response window (e.g., <1 second). Concurrently, it launches an asynchronous, comprehensive evidence-gathering and synthesis process across diverse sources. The initial answer is clearly labeled as provisional and is dynamically updated or enriched as the background process completes, with users able to view the integrated evidence trail and request clarifications on specific points. This maintains engagement by delivering immediate utility while fulfilling the original goal of a more thorough, multi-evidence response.

ideas.title: Provenance-Aware Visualization with Statistical Guardrails for Rigorous Exploration
ideas.description: This system develops interactive visualization tools that actively prevent statistical misuse and promote reproducible research. It integrates real-time statistical diagnostics that warn users about common pitfalls like multiple testing, p-hacking, and data dredging as they interact with and filter data. All exploration steps—including filters, selections, and transformations—are automatically captured in a detailed, exportable provenance log to ensure the analytical path is transparent and reproducible. The result is a powerful exploratory environment that balances interactive freedom with methodological rigor, guiding users toward more valid and defensible conclusions.

ideas.title: Diversity-Aware, Counterfactual Annotation for Academic Search Using Multi-Profile Collaborative Filtering
ideas.description: This system captures user interaction patterns to build multiple latent researcher profiles per user, representing their different research interests or roles. For a query, it identifies both similar and strategically dissimilar profile clusters. Search result annotations are generated through a weighted mechanism that balances the popularity of a result within a user's primary similarity cluster against its significance to complementary, external clusters. This creates annotations such as 'Frequently selected in your core field' and 'Highly relevant to interdisciplinary approaches in [related field]', promoting discovery while maintaining relevance.

ideas.title: A Federated, Latency-Optimized Compute Fabric for Distributed Bioinformatics
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid bioinformatics compute architecture that treats user-owned devices (running lightweight local clients) and institutional server clusters as nodes in a federated, latency-sensitive compute market. Inspired by high-frequency trading algorithms, a central coordinator uses ultra-low-latency scheduling to dynamically partition and auction HMMER-like search jobs. This allows a user's local device to handle sensitive or high-priority query segments for immediate control, while seamlessly offloading computationally intensive, non-sensitive batch segments to the optimal available server resource in real-time, creating a load-balanced system that respects user sovereignty over data.

ideas.title: Precision-First Visual Exploration: Coupling Interactive Visualizations with Dynamic, Queryable Data Tables
ideas.description: This system reimagines the search results interface as a coordinated visual and numeric workspace, where interactive visualizations serve as the primary discovery canvas. A dynamic data table, generated from the same underlying result set, is always accessible as a linked, detail-on-demand panel. User interactions in the visualization (e.g., selecting a bar in a histogram) immediately filter and highlight corresponding rows in the table, and vice-versa, creating a tight bidirectional coupling. This design ensures that the exploratory power of visualization is fully retained while permanently grounding the experience in the precise, verifiable data necessary for rigorous analysis.

ideas.title: Hardware-Accelerated Remote Workflow APIs with Dynamic Vectorization
ideas.description: This research proposes extending REST APIs for complex workflows to include metadata and control mechanisms for hardware-specific vectorization. The API would allow a client to define a multi-step workflow where intermediate results, such as large vectors or matrices from a search step, can be processed by optimized kernels (like the striped MSV) on available hardware accelerators. The server dynamically selects the optimal instruction set (AVX-512) or offloads to a GPU based on data size and type, treating the accelerator as a managed computational resource within the workflow chain. This enables high-performance, data-dependent parallel processing to become a seamlessly orchestrated service within standard web service architectures.

ideas.title: Secure Enclave Execution of Community Query Models on Private Databases
ideas.description: This system enables collaborative analysis while guaranteeing data sovereignty. Users publish standardized HMM query profiles to a community repository. To execute a search, a user downloads a chosen query model and runs it securely within a local, attested trusted execution environment (like Intel SGX or a secure container) that houses their private database. The model executes in isolation, and its outputs are automatically filtered through differential privacy mechanisms before any result leaves the enclave. This design ensures the private database is never exposed, the query model cannot exfiltrate data, and the user retains final authorization over any result sharing.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Multi-Perspective Visualization for Large-Scale Data Exploration
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from astronomy, specifically tools like the WorldWide Telescope, to the challenge of interactively visualizing large scientific result sets. The analogous problem in astronomy involves making petabyte-scale, multi-instrument sky surveys explorable. That field solved it by creating unified, real-time interfaces for panning, zooming, and layering data from different sources. The adapted idea is to develop a similar interactive visualization tool for general research data. This framework would treat large result sets as a cohesive 'data sky,' allowing users to dynamically filter, adjust visualization dimensions, and overlay different analytical views or data subsets in real time. The goal is to enable intuitive, context-preserving exploration of complex data, revealing patterns and relationships that are difficult to see in static summaries or isolated visualizations.

ideas.title: Apply Ant Colony Optimization for Dynamic Query Routing in Server Clusters
ideas.description: This idea adapts Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) from swarm intelligence to solve the ultra-low-latency scheduling problem for batch query queues. In the analogous biological system, ants use pheromone trails to converge on optimal paths. The adapted solution implements a digital pheromone map across the server cluster, where each successfully processed query reinforces the 'trail' to its processing node with a strength based on latency. An ACO-inspired scheduler then routes incoming batch queries probabilistically, favoring paths (server nodes) with the strongest recent pheromone signals (indicating low latency and high availability). This creates a decentralized, self-organizing load-balancing system that dynamically and rapidly adapts to changing server loads and query complexities, optimizing overall cluster throughput without a centralized bottleneck.

ideas.title: Adaptive Real-Time Data Display Using a Biological Signal Processing Model
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience, where real-time EEG/MEG signal processing pipelines analyze and visualize brain activity as it occurs. The analogous system uses adaptive filtering and incremental feature extraction on a continuous data stream to produce live visualizations like updating topographic maps. Adapted to the research context, we propose implementing a back-end stream processing architecture where partial results from a distributed computation are treated as a continuous signal. The pipeline will perform incremental aggregation, statistical summarization, and feature extraction on these streaming result packets. This enables a front-end display that updates adaptively in real-time, showing the evolving progress, intermediate findings, and convergence trends of the computation, much like a live monitor for a scientific experiment.

ideas.title: Hybrid Stream-Batch Architecture with a Consistency Frontier for Verifiable Real-Time Results
ideas.description: This system combines a low-latency streaming front-end with a high-fidelity batch back-end to guarantee both real-time previews and deterministic final results. The streaming layer computes and emits partial results immediately for user display, while all computations are simultaneously logged for deterministic re-execution in a batch layer. A 'consistency frontier' mechanism marks results as provisional until the batch layer validates them, after which they become immutable and final. The user interface and API explicitly differentiate between provisional and finalized data, ensuring users benefit from real-time feedback without ever sacrificing verifiable correctness.

ideas.title: Privacy-Preserving, User-Sovereign Search Profiles with Client-Side Encryption
ideas.description: This system enables saving and sharing of searches without exposing sensitive user data. All search histories, results, and custom workflows are encrypted and digitally signed on the client side before any data is transmitted or stored on the server. Users authenticate via decentralized, self-sovereign identity (e.g., using W3C Verifiable Credentials), allowing them to share specific, attested search profiles or workflows with others without relying on the central service as an identity provider. The server facilitates synchronization and sharing of the encrypted data blobs but cannot read or correlate the sensitive contents, fundamentally mitigating the risks of data breaches and privacy violations inherent in a traditional user account model.

ideas.title: Ultra-Low-Latency Vector-Aware Load Balancing for Heterogeneous Compute Clusters
ideas.description: This research combines the ultra-low-latency, queue-optimizing algorithms from high-frequency trading with advanced, hardware-specific vectorization techniques. The core idea is to develop a cluster scheduler that maintains a real-time, fine-grained model of each server's vector processing capabilities (SIMD width, GPU availability, memory bandwidth). Incoming batch queries or computational tasks are dynamically profiled for their vectorization potential, and the load-balancer then routes and partitions them across the cluster not just for even load, but to maximize hardware-specific vector throughput. This results in a system that minimizes end-to-end latency by co-optimizing for queue scheduling delay and the execution efficiency of the underlying vector units.

Clients submit workflow jobs asynchronously and receive a durable handle for polling status, querying intermediate results, and managing execution. The engine guarantees reliability through persisted state, configurable retry logic, idempotent operations, and support for compensating transactions to unwind side effects upon failure, making multi-step remote workflows robust and manageable.
